tests.instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question:
Find the energy of the photon which has a wavelength of 0.50 $\mathop A\limits^ \circ $ ?

(Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 $\times$ 10^(-34) J)
questions.question_html:
Find the energy of the photon which has a wavelength of 0.50 {tex}\mathop A\limits^ \circ {/tex}?
(Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 J)
questions.explanation:
Energy (E) of a photon having wavelength ( $\lambda$ ) is given by the expression, $E={hc\over \lambda}$ 

where, h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 $\times$ 10^(-34)

c = velocity of light in vacuum = 3 $\times$ 10^(8) m/s 

Wavelength $\lambda = 0.50 A^° = 0.50\times 10^{-10} m$ $\lambda$ = 0.50 Å = 0.50 $\times$ 10^(-10) m

Substituting the values in the given expression of E:

 $6.626\times 10^{-34}\times 3\times 10^8\over 0.50\times 10^{-10}$ = 3.98 $\times$ 10^(-15) J

questions.question: Assign oxidation number to P in NaH_(2)PO_(4.)
questions.question_html: Assign oxidation number to P in NaH2PO4.
questions.explanation:
The oxidation state of P in NaH_(2)PO_(4 )is +5.

Let the oxidation number of P be x. As we already know the oxidation number of other atoms so we can calculate that of P too.

Na = +1

H= +1 (+2 for hydrogen molecule)

O = -2

Now calculating oxidation number of P:-

1 +2 + x +(-8) = 0

On solving we get x = +5.

questions.question: A molecule or ion is stable if:
questions.question_html: A molecule or ion is stable if:
questions.explanation:
Stability of molecule: It is determined by bond order. Higher is the bond order greater is the stability of the molecule.

Bond order is defined as the number of covalent bonds between the two combining atoms of a molecule.

Bond order = 0.5 (N_(b) – N_(a))

If N_(b) > N_(a), then the molecule will be stable.

N_(b) = number of bonding electrons or number of electrons in bonding M.O’s.

N_(a) = number of antibonding electrons.

questions.question: The oxidizing power of halogens increase in the order of:
questions.question_html: The oxidizing power of halogens increase in the order of:
questions.explanation:
Halogens have high electronegativity and electron affinity. They have a greater tendency to accept electrons or easily reduced, therefore they are a strong oxidizing agent.

As the reduction potential decrease down the group, the oxidizing power decrease down the group the order of the oxidizing power will be as under

F_(2) > Cl_(2)> Br_(2)> I_(2)

The oxidizing power depends on,

The heat of dissociation of halogen molecule.

Electron affinity of the atom.

Hydration energy of the ion.

Heat of vaporization

If a halogen has low energy of dissociation, a high electron affinity, and higher hydration of its ion, it will have high oxidizing power.

F has although low electron affinity than Cl but low dissociation energy and has high hydration energy of its ion, therefore Fluorine is the strongest oxidizing agent.

questions.question_html: The fragrance of flowers is due to the presence of some steam volatile organic compounds called essential oils. These are generally insoluble in water at room temperature but are miscible with water vapour in vapour phase. A suitable method for the extraction of these oils from the flowers is:
questions.explanation: Steam distillation is a technique applied to separate substances which are steam volatile and are immiscible with water. Essential oils are insoluble in water and soluble in steam and have high vapour pressure. Therefore, they can be separated by steam distillation.

questions.question: According to the quantum-theoretical model of an atom, each orbital is designated by three quantum numbers labelled as n, l, and m​​​​​​. These are referred to respectively as:
questions.question_html: According to the quantum-theoretical model of an atom, each orbital is designated by three quantum numbers labelled as n, l, and m​​​​​​. These are referred to respectively as:

options.text: Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, Principal quantum number, and spin quantum number.
options.html: Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, Principal quantum number, and spin quantum number.

options.text: Principal quantum number, Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, and Magnetic orbital quantum number.
options.html: Principal quantum number, Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, and Magnetic orbital quantum number.

options.text: Principal quantum number, Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, and spin quantum number
options.html: Principal quantum number, Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, and spin quantum number

options.text: Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, Principal quantum number, and Magnetic orbital quantum number.
options.html: Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, Principal quantum number, and Magnetic orbital quantum number.

correct_options: Principal quantum number, Azimuthal quantum number or orbital angular momentum, and Magnetic orbital quantum number.
questions.explanation:
Quantum numbers designate specific shells, subshells, orbitals, and spins of electrons. 

There are a total of four quantum numbers:

 The principal quantum number (n), describes the energy of an electron and the most probable distance of the electron from the nucleus. In other words, it refers to the size of the orbital and the energy level an electron is placed in.
 The orbital angular momentum quantum number (l), describes the shape of the orbital. It can also be used to determine the number of angular nodes.
 The magnetic quantum number (m), describes the energy levels in a subshell.
 The electron spin quantum number (ms) refers to the spin on the electron, which can either be up or down.

questions.question:
Thiosulphate reacts differently with iodine and bromine in the reactions given below:

 $2 \mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}+\mathrm{I}_{2}$ $\rightarrow \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{I}^-$ 

 $\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}$ + 2Br_(2) + 5H_(2)O $\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ + 2Br^(-) + 10H^(+)

Which of the following statements justifies the above dual behavior of thiosulphate?
questions.question_html:
Thiosulphate reacts differently with iodine and bromine in the reactions given below:
{tex}2 \mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}+\mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{I}^-{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} + 2Br2 + 5H2O {tex}\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} + 2Br- + 10H+
Which of the following statements justifies the above dual behavior of thiosulphate?

options.text: Bromine undergoes oxidation and iodine undergoes reduction in these reactions.
options.html: Bromine undergoes oxidation and iodine undergoes reduction in these reactions.

options.text: Bromine is a weaker oxidant than iodine.
options.html: Bromine is a weaker oxidant than iodine.

options.text: Thiosulphate undergoes oxidation by bromine and reduction by iodine in these reactions.
options.html: Thiosulphate undergoes oxidation by bromine and reduction by iodine in these reactions.

options.text: Bromine is a stronger oxidant than iodine.
options.html: Bromine is a stronger oxidant than iodine.

correct_options: Bromine is a stronger oxidant than iodine.
questions.explanation:
$2 \mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{I}_{2}^{0}(\mathrm{s})$ $\rightarrow{2.5} \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}$ (aq) + 2I^(-) (aq)

 $\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}$ (aq) + 2Br_(2) (I) + 5H_(2)O(I) $\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-} $ (aq) + 4Br^(-) (aq) + 10H^(+) (aq)

Bromine being stronger oxidizing agent than I_(2), therefore it oxidises S of $\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}$ to higher oxidation state +6 $ \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-} $ whereas I_(2) oxidises it only into $ \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}$ ion.

questions.question: In NO^(-)_(3) ion, the number of bond pairs and lone pairs of electrons on nitrogen atom are
questions.question_html: In NO-3 ion, the number of bond pairs and lone pairs of electrons on nitrogen atom are
questions.explanation:
The nitrate ion is formed by the loss of the hydrogen ion, and so its structure is:

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/cbse/review/Ques_21429.jpg) 

Around the central nitrogen there are 4 pairs of shared electrons, and no remaining lone pair. The original lone pair has now become a bonding pair. Two of those pairs make up a double bond. The double bond unit and the two single bonds arrange themselves as far apart as possible in a trigonal planar arrangement - exactly the same as the carbonate ion.

questions.question: A qualitative measure of the stability of an ionic compound is provided by:
questions.question_html: A qualitative measure of the stability of an ionic compound is provided by:
questions.explanation: Lattice energy is an estimate of the bond strength in ionic compounds. It is defined as the heat of formation for ions of opposite charge in the gas phase to combine into an ionic solid. The stability of the ionic bond is directly proportional to lattice energy.

questions.question: Which one of the following compounds is used in the estimation of oxygen?
questions.question_html: Which one of the following compounds is used in the estimation of oxygen?
questions.explanation: I_(2)O_(5) is used. A definite mass of an organic compound is decomposed by heating in a stream of nitrogen gas. The mixture of gaseous products containing oxygen is passed over red-hot coke when all the oxygen is converted to carbon monoxide. This mixture is passed through warm iodine pentoxide when carbon monoxide is oxidised to carbon dioxide producing iodine.

questions.question: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of a substance A to 18 g of water. Calculate the mass percent of the solute?
questions.question_html: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of a substance A to 18 g of water. Calculate the mass percent of the solute?
questions.explanation:
Mass percent is calculated using the expression,

Mass percent = ${ Weight \ of\ solute \over Weight\ of \ solution }\times 100$ 

 = $ {weight\ of\ solute \over weight\ of \ solute\ + \ weight\ of \ solvent }\times 100$ 

Substituting the given values in the above expression we get,

Mass percent = ${ 2 \over 2 +18 }\times 100 $ 

= ${ 2 \over 20 }\times 100 $ 

= 10.00 %

questions.question: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 $\times$ 10^(15) Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 $\times$ 10^(-34) Js)?
questions.question_html: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js)?
questions.explanation:
We know Planck's equation is

E = hv

where E is energy, h is Planck’s constant and v is frequency.

E = 6.626 $\times$ 10^(-34) $\times$ 3 $\times$ 10^(15 )= 1.988 $\times$ 10^(-18) J

questions.question:
^(--) $Ba_3N_2$ +^(-) $H_2O$ → ^(--) Ba $(OH)_2$ + ^(--) $NH_3$ .

The above equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces from LHS to RHS respectively.
questions.question_html:
-- {tex}Ba_3N_2{/tex} +- {tex}H_2O{/tex} → -- Ba {tex}(OH)_2{/tex} + -- {tex}NH_3{/tex} .

The above equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces from LHS to RHS respectively.
questions.explanation:
The given equation gets stoichiometrically balanced when the coefficients ( 1, 6, 3, 2 ) starting from LHS to RHS are inserted in given blank spaces.

Thus, the balanced equation for the reaction is,

Ba_(3)N_(2) + 6H_(2)O $\longrightarrow$ 3Ba(OH)_(2) + 2NH_(3)

questions.question: The mass of an electron is
questions.question_html: The mass of an electron is
questions.explanation:
The electron is a subatomic particle, symbol e^(−) or $\beta^-$ , with a negative elementary electric charge.

The electron has a mass that is approximately $1\over 1836$ that of the proton.

So mass of electron = ${1\over 1836}\times 1.6726219 × 10^{-27}$ = 9.1094 $\times$ 10^(-31) kg

questions.question: Dihydrogen reacts with dioxygen to form water. The reaction is:
questions.question_html: Dihydrogen reacts with dioxygen to form water. The reaction is:
questions.explanation:
The formation of water from its elements produces, in addition to water, a tremendous amount of energy, 572 kJ to be exact.

2H_(2) + O_(2) $\to$ 2H_(2)O + Energy

This is an example of an exothermic reaction, a reaction that produces energy. It is also an example of what is called a combustion reaction, where a substance (in this case hydrogen gas) is combined with oxygen.

questions.question: A covalent bond when cleaved heterolytically yields:
questions.question_html: A covalent bond when cleaved heterolytically yields:
questions.explanation: Heterolytic cleavage produces carbonium ion and carbanion. In heterolytic cleavage, the bond breaks in such a fashion that the shared pair of electrons remains with one of the fragments. After heterolysis, one atom has a sextet electronic structure and a positive charge and the other, a valence octet with at least one lone pair and a negative charge. A species having a carbon atom possessing electrons and a positive charge is called a carbocation (earlier called carbonium ion). There is also a carbon species carrying a negative charge on a carbon atom which gets the unshared pair of electrons which is called carbanion.

questions.question: 2 $\times$ 10^(8) atoms of carbon are arranged side by side. Find the radius of carbon atom if the length of this arrangement is 2.4 cm.
questions.question_html: 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 atoms of carbon are arranged side by side. Find the radius of carbon atom if the length of this arrangement is 2.4 cm.
questions.explanation:
Total Length = 2.4 cm

Total number of atoms along the length = 2 $\times$ 10^(8)

Therefore diameter of each atom = $2.4 \over 2 × 10^8$ = 1.2 $\times$ 10^(-8 )cm

And radius of the atom = $ 1.2 \times 10^{-8}\over 2$ = 0.60 $\times$ 10^(-8) cm 

= 6.0 $\times$ 10^(-11)m

questions.question: Considering the elements B, C, N, F and Si, the correct order of their non-metallic character is:
questions.question_html: Considering the elements B, C, N, F and Si, the correct order of their non-metallic character is:
questions.explanation:
In a period, the non-metallic character increases from left to right. Thus, among B, C, N and F, non-metallic character decreases in the order: F > N > C > B. However, within a group, non-metallic character decreases from top to bottom. Thus, C is more non-metallic than Si. Therefore, the correct sequence of decreasing non-metallic character is: F > N > C > B > Si.

The same can be verified with first ionization potential of the atoms. The values are F (17.42) > N (14.53) > C (11.26) > B (8.298) > Si (8.151). Lower ionization potential implies easy tendency to loose electrons. The potential of B and Si appears to be close but it is the bigger size of Si that helps it loose electron comparatively faster.

questions.question: The ${\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}{{\text{d}}^{\text{2}}}$ hybridization of central atom of a molecule would lead to
questions.question_html: The {tex}{\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}{{\text{d}}^{\text{2}}}{/tex} hybridization of central atom of a molecule would lead to
questions.explanation:

questions.question: Correct expression for all series of lines in the hydrogen spectrum is (n_(1) = 1, 2 , 3...) and n_(2) = n_(1) + 1, n_(1) + 2, ..., and $\bar \nu $ is the wave number)
questions.question_html: Correct expression for all series of lines in the hydrogen spectrum is (n1 = 1, 2 , 3...) and n2 = n1 + 1, n1 + 2, ..., and {tex}\bar \nu {/tex} is the wave number)
questions.explanation:
In an amazing demonstration of mathematical insight, in 1885 Balmer came up with a simple formula for predicting the wavelength of any of the lines in atomic Hydrogen in what we now know as the Balmer series.

Three years later, Rydberg generalized this so that it was possible to determine the wavelengths of any of the lines in the hydrogen emission spectrum. Rydberg suggested that all atomic spectra formed families with this pattern (he was unaware of Balmer's work).

It turns out that there are families of spectra following Rydberg's pattern, notably in the alkali metals, sodium, potassium, etc., but not with the precision the hydrogen atom lines fit the Balmer formula, and low values of n_(2) predicted wavelengths that deviate considerably.

Rydberg's general equation is as follows:

 $\overline \nu = {1\over \lambda}=R_h\left ({1\over n_1^2}-{1\over n_2^2}\right)$ 

where

 R_(H) is the Rydberg constant and is 1,09,677 cm^(-1)
 n_(1) and n_(2) are integers (whole numbers) with n_(2)>n_(1)

questions.question: Which color of light has the greatest energy per photon?

questions.explanation: Waves with a short wavelength have the most energy. Red waves have a relatively long wavelength (in the 700 nm range), and violet waves are much shorter - roughly half that. Because violet waves have the shortest wavelength of the visible light spectrum, they carry the most energy.

questions.question: According to Bohr’s theory the frequency of radiation absorbed or emitted when transition occurs between two stationary states that differ in energy by $\Delta {\text{E}},$ is given by
questions.question_html: According to Bohr’s theory the frequency of radiation absorbed or emitted when transition occurs between two stationary states that differ in energy by {tex}\Delta {\text{E}},{/tex} is given by
questions.explanation:
In his model of the atom, Bohr used Planck's quantum hypothesis, and of course his knowledge from prior findings. Bohr uses Ryberg's formula for explaining how the electrons emit light as they move from one orbital to another. The point Bohr was trying to get across is that energy is not continuous in an atom. We can say that an atom that is in the lowest energy level is in the ground state, and when it moves to a higher level it is in an excited state. The energy of a photon, lost or gained, is calculated using Planck's equation: (h is Planck's constant, 6.62607 $\times$ 10^(-34 )J s / cycles, and v stands for frequency in cycles/s)

Energy Difference( $\Delta$ E) = $h\nu$

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
Once an organic compound is extracted from a natural source or synthesised in the laboratory, it is essential to purify it. Various methods used for the purification of organic compounds are based on the nature of the compound and the impurity present in it. Finally, the purity of a compound is ascertained by determining its melting or boiling point. This is one of the most commonly used techniques for the purification of solid organic compounds. In crystallisation Impurities, which impart colour to the solution are removed by adsorbing over activated charcoal. In distillation Liquids having different boiling points vaporise at different temperatures. The vapours are cooled and the liquids so formed are collected separately. Steam Distillation is applied to separate substances which are steam volatile and are immiscible with water. Distillation under reduced pressure: This method is used to purify liquids having very high boiling points.

Assertion (A): Crystallisation is based on the difference in the solubilities of the compound and the impurities in a suitable solvent.

Reason (R): The impure compound is dissolved in a solvent.

questions.question: Select the species which are iso-electronic (same number of the electron) with each other.
questions.question_html: Select the species which are iso-electronic (same number of the electron) with each other.
questions.explanation:
Neutral Cl ( Z=17) has 17 electrons and with the addition of one more electron, it becomes Cl^(-) anion which has 18 electrons.

Neutral Ca (Z=20) has 20 electrons and with the removal of 2 electrons, it becomes Ca^(2+) cation which has 18 electrons.

Since Cl^(- ) and Ca^(2+) both has 18 electrons, so they are isoelectronic.

questions.question:
Water behaves as an amphoteric substance because:

 it has the ability to act as an acid.
 it has the ability to act as a base.
 it has the ability to act as an acid as well as a base.
 it has the ability to oxidize as well as reduce.
questions.question_html:
Water behaves as an amphoteric substance because:

  1. it has the ability to act as an acid.
  2. it has the ability to act as a base.
  3. it has the ability to act as an acid as well as a base.
  4. it has the ability to oxidize as well as reduce.
questions.explanation:
An amphoteric compound is a compound that can act either as an acid or as a base in a chemical reaction. The first example of water's amphoteric character is the fact that it can self-ionize to a small extent. A water molecule is capable of donating one of its protons (H^(+)) to a neighbouring water molecule, forming the hydroxide and hydronium ions.

 $H_2O+H_2O\rightleftharpoons H_3O^++OH^-$

questions.question: According to Gay Lussac’s Law of Gaseous Volumes, " when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction they do so in a ______ ratio by volumes provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure."
questions.question_html: According to Gay Lussac’s Law of Gaseous Volumes, " when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction they do so in a ______ ratio  by volumes provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure."
questions.explanation:
Gay lussac’s law states that when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction they do so in a simple ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.

Mathematically, the law is expressed as,

[V/n] = k

where V represents the volume of the gas, n denotes the number of moles of the gas & k is the proportionality constant.

Thus, the volume ratio must be related to the relative number of molecules.

This relationship was crucial in establishing the formula of simple molecules at a time when a distinction between atoms and molecules was not clearly understood.

questions.explanation:
Cathode rays (also called an electron beam or e-beam) are streams of electrons observed in vacuum tubes. If an evacuated glass tube is equipped with two electrodes and a voltage is applied, the glass behind of the positive electrode is observed to glow, due to electrons emitted from and traveling away from the cathode (the electrode connected to the negative terminal of the voltage supply).

Cathode ray tubes (CRTs) use a focused beam of electrons deflected by electric or magnetic fields to create the image in a television set.

questions.question: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.question_html: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.explanation:
A common characteristic of most Alkali metal is their ability to displace H_(2) (g) from water. This is represented by their large, negative electrode potentials. In this event, the Group 1 metal is oxidized to its metal ion, and water is reduced to form hydrogen gas and hydroxide ions. The general reaction of an alkali metal (M) with H_(2)O (l) is given in the following equation:

2M(s) + 2H_(2)O(l) $\longrightarrow$ 2M + (aq) + 2OH−(aq) + H_(2)(g)

From this reaction it is apparent that OH- is produced, creating a basic or alkaline environment. Group 1 elements are called alkali metals because of their ability to displace H_(2) (g) from water and create a basic solution. Alkali metals are also known to react violently and explosively with water. This is because enough heat is given off during the exothermic reaction to ignite the H_(2)(g).

questions.question_html: The Kelvin scale is related to celsius scale as:
questions.explanation:
The relation between the Kelvin scale & Celcius scale of temperatures is -

K = °C + 273.15

Such a relationship is based upon the experimental findings and subsequent conclusion of Charle's law.

The Kelvin scale is also termed as an "absolute scale of temperature."

It is interesting to note that temperature below 0^(o)C ( ie. negative values ) is possible in the Celcius scale but in Kelvin scale, the negative temperature is not possible.

options.text: the element is present in different oxidation states.
options.html: the element is present in different oxidation states.

options.text: middle carbon in such elements has always zero oxidation state.
options.html: middle carbon in such elements has always zero oxidation state.

options.text: the average oxidation state of the element is divided into two.
options.html: the average oxidation state of the element is divided into two.

options.text: electrons are never shared/transferred in that element.
options.html: electrons are never shared/transferred in that element.

correct_options: the element is present in different oxidation states.
questions.explanation:
The oxidation states are usually in whole numbers, but in some cases, they are in fractional numbers. Consider Fe_(3)O_(4).

 Using rule #2, the sum of the oxidation state of all atoms is 0
 Using rule #6, the O has the oxidation state of -2. 4 $\times$ (-2) = -8
 Equation: 3Fe + (-8) = 0
 3Fe = 8
 Fe = $8 \over 3$ 

In most cases, atoms of the same element in a given compound have the same oxidation states, but each atom can have a different state than the other. For Fe_(3)O_(4), two Fe atoms have an oxidation state of +3 and one of +2, which makes the total oxidation state of Fe= $8 \over 3$ .

Instead of averaging the oxidation states, we must keep them separated. This is called fragmenting, which occurs if there is an ionic compound, and the ions can be separated. The oxidation state of the individual atoms in the different ions can be determined.

questions.question: In sulphur estimation, 0.157 g of an organic compound gave 0.4813 g of barium sulphate. What is the percentage of sulphur in the compound?
questions.question_html: In sulphur estimation, 0.157 g of an organic compound gave 0.4813 g of barium sulphate. What is the percentage of sulphur in the compound?
questions.explanation:
Molecular mass of BaSO_(4 )= 137 + 32 + 64 g = 233 g

233 g BaSO_(4) contains 32 g S

0.4813 g BaSO_(4 )contains $\frac{{(32 \times 0.4813g)}}{{233\;S}}$ 

So % of S = $ \frac{{(32 \times 0.4813 \times 100)}}{{(233 \times 0.157)}}$ 

S = 42.10%

questions.question: During the formation of a chemical bond:

options.text: energy of the system does not change
options.html: energy of the system does not change

options.text: electron-electron repulsion becomes more than the nucleus-electron attraction
options.html: electron-electron repulsion becomes more than the nucleus-electron attraction

questions.explanation: A chemical bond is a lasting attraction between atoms that enables the formation of chemical compounds. The bond may result from the electrostatic force of attraction between atoms with opposite charges, or through the sharing of electrons as in the covalent bonds. When a bond forms, electrons are attracted to the space between nuclei where the electrostatic force of attraction is greater. As the electrons fall to a position of lower potential energy, the total mechanical energy of the molecular system decreases. Part of the mechanical energy of the unbound atoms is lost when they form the molecular system with a lower total mechanical energy.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
The phenomenon of the existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers. Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures (manners in which atoms are linked) are classified as structural isomers. Structural isomers are classified as chain isomer, position isomer, functional group isomer. Meristematic arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule and stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism. Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of σ electrons of the C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of an unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital. This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent σ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.

Assertion (A): Isopentane, pentane and Neopentane are chain isomers.

Reason (R): They have a similar molecular formula but a different carbon skeleton.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
The phenomenon of the existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers. Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures (manners in which atoms are linked) are classified as structural isomers. Structural isomers are classified as chain isomer, position isomer, functional group isomer. Meristematic arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule and stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism. Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of σ electrons of the C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of an unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital. This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent σ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.

Assertion (A): C_(4)H_(10)O represents methoxypropane (CH_(3)OC_(3)H_(7)) and ethoxyethane (C_(2)H_(5)OC_(2)H_(5)). 

Reason (R): Stereoisomerism can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism.
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:

The phenomenon of the existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers. Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures (manners in which atoms are linked) are classified as structural isomers. Structural isomers are classified as chain isomer, position isomer, functional group isomer. Meristematic arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule and stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism. Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of σ electrons of the C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of an unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital. This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent σ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.


Assertion (A): C4H10O represents methoxypropane (CH3OC3H7) and ethoxyethane (C2H5OC2H5). 
Reason (R): Stereoisomerism can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism.

tests.instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: Among halogens, the correct order of amount of energy released in electron gain (electron gain enthalpy) is:
questions.question_html: Among halogens, the correct order of amount of energy released in electron gain (electron gain enthalpy) is:
questions.explanation:
Chlorine has a higher electron gain enthalpy than fluorine. This is due to the small size of fluorine atom, i.e., the electron density is high which resists the addition of an electron (F < CI) In general, electron gain enthalpy decreases as atomic size increases. Thus, electron gain enthalpy follows the order: CI > Br > I.

F, Cl, Br, I have electron gain enthalpy value -328, -349, -325, and -295 respectively.

questions.question: Wavenumber of yellow radiations having a wavelength of 5800 $\overset{\circ }{\mathop{A}}\,$ is:
questions.question_html: Wavenumber of yellow radiations having a wavelength of 5800 {tex}\overset{\circ }{\mathop{A}}\,{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Wavenumber is defined as the reciprocal of a wavelength.

 $\bar \nu ={1\over \lambda}$ 

where, $\lambda $ = wavelength = 5800 Å = 5.8 $\times$ 10^(-7) m

So, $\bar \nu ={1\over 5.8\times 10^{-7}}= $ 1.72 $\times$ 10^(6) m^(-1).

questions.question: In a period, the lowest first ionization energy is exhibited by:
questions.question_html: In a period, the lowest first ionization energy is exhibited by:
questions.explanation: The first ionization energy is the energy required to remove one mole of the most loosely held electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms to produce 1 mole of gaseous ions each with a charge of 1+. Alkali earth metals have the lowest ionization energy in their respective periods, due to their general electronic configuration (ns^(1)).

questions.question: The criterion of overlap of atomic orbitals is the main factor for the formation of:
questions.question_html: The criterion of overlap of atomic orbitals is the main factor for the formation of:
questions.explanation:
The valence bond theory is introduced to describe the bonding in organic molecules. In this model, bonds are considered to form from the overlapping of two atomic orbitals on different atoms, each orbital containing a single electron.

In general, greater the overlap, stronger is the bond formed between the two atoms. Thus, according to the orbital overlap concept, atoms combine together by overlapping their orbital and thus forming a lower energy state where their valence electrons with opposite spin, pair up to form the covalent bond.

questions.question: The standard emf of a galvanic cell involving 2 moles of electrons in its redox reaction is 0.59 V. The equilibrium constant for the redox reaction of the cell is______.
questions.question_html: The standard emf of a galvanic cell involving 2 moles of electrons in its redox reaction is 0.59 V. The equilibrium constant for the redox reaction of the cell is______.
questions.explanation:
constant K_(c) can be calculated as

here, no. of moles of electrons = 2

 $E^0cell = {0.059 \over n}logKc$ or $0.59 = {0.059 \over 2}logKc$ 

or $logKc= {0.59\times2 \over 0.059}= 20$ 

Kc = antilog 20 = 10^(20)

Kc = 10^(20)

questions.question:
Which of the following statements about hydrogen are correct?

 The hydrogen ion, H^(+), exists freely in solution.
 Dihydrogen does not act as a reducing agent.
 Hydrogen has three isotopes of which protium is the most common.
 Hydrogen never acts as an anion in ionic salts.
questions.question_html:
Which of the following statements about hydrogen are correct?

  1. The hydrogen ion, H+, exists freely in solution.
  2. Dihydrogen does not act as a reducing agent.
  3. Hydrogen has three isotopes of which protium is the most common.
  4. Hydrogen never acts as an anion in ionic salts.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen has three naturally occurring isotopes: protium, deuterium, and tritium. Each isotope has different chemical properties. Protium is the most prevalent hydrogen isotope, with an abundance of 99.98%. It consists of one proton and one electron. It is typically not found in its monoatomic form, but bonded with itself (H_(2)) or other elements.

questions.question: When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions, what happens?
questions.question_html: When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions, what happens?

options.text: inductive and electromeric effects operate with the same magnitude.
options.html: inductive and electromeric effects operate with the same magnitude.

questions.explanation: Electromeric Effect (E effect) is a temporary effect. The organic compounds having multiple bonds (a double or triple bond) show this effect in the presence of an attacking reagent only. It is defined as the complete transfer of a shared pair of $\pi$ -electrons to one of the atoms joined by multiple bonds on the demand of an attacking reagent. Polarisation of $\sigma$ -bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent $\sigma$ -bond is referred to as the inductive effect. When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions then electromeric effect predominates.

questions.explanation:
In the Dumas method for estimation of nitrogen in an organic compound, the nitrogen-containing organic compound, when heated with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide, yields free nitrogen in addition to carbon dioxide and water.

 $C_xH_yN_z\;+\;\left(x+\frac y2\right)CuO\rightarrow xCO_2\;+\frac y2\;H_2O\;+\frac z2N_2\;+\left(2x+\frac y2\right)Cu$

questions.explanation: By definition, the oxidation number of an atom is the charge that an atom would have if the compound was composed of ions. The oxidation number of simple ions is equal to the charge on the ion. The oxidation number of sodium in the Na^(+) ion is +1, for example, and the oxidation number of chlorine in the Cl^(-) ion is -1.

questions.question: What is the mass of 1 molecule of O_(2)?
questions.question_html: What is the mass of 1 molecule of O2?
questions.explanation:
Calculations: Since the molar mass of O_(2 )= 32 g / mole

 $\therefore$ 1 mol of O_(2) gas containing 6.022 $\times$ 10^(23 ) molecules have a mass = 32.0 g

so 1 molecule would have a mass

= [ 32 / ( 6.022 $\times$ 10^(23) ) ] g

= 5.3138 $\times$ 10^(-23) g

Thus, the mass of one molecule of O_(2) = 5.32 $\times$ 10^(-23) g

2N2H4(l) + N2O4(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3N2 (g) + 4H2O (l),
Which is the limiting reagent for this reaction?

For the given reaction,

2N_(2)H_(4 )(l) + N_(2)O_(4 )(l) $\rightarrow$ 3N_(2) (g) + 4H_(2)O (l)

as per its stoichiometry, if only the right and exact amount of N_(2)O_(4) is taken, the reaction may not go to completion. As such it would be consumed first & is, therefore, the limiting reagent.

questions.question: Which one of the following is paramagnetic?
questions.question_html: Which one of the following is paramagnetic?
questions.explanation: The number of electrons in Nitrogen is 7 and in oxygen is 8. Hence the number of electrons that would be present in the molecular orbitals in NO is 7 + 8 = 15. As the number of electrons is odd, all the electrons in the NO molecule cannot be paired. Hence, a single electron would be present in a $\pi$ *2p orbital. Therefore NO is an odd electron species and the gas is hence paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electron.

questions.explanation: Hydrogen peroxide has been used to reduce the BOD and COD of industrial wastewaters for many years. H_(2)O_(2) can be used alone or with catalysts such as iron, UV light, ozone (O_(3)), and alkali to oxidize BOD/COD contributing compounds in wastewaters.

questions.question_html: Rank the bonds in the set C=O, C-O, C {tex} \equiv {/tex} O in order of decreasing bond length.
questions.explanation: The length of the bond is determined by the number of bonded electrons (the bond order). The higher the bond order, the stronger the pull between the two atoms and the shorter the bond length. Generally, the length of the bond between two atoms is approximately the sum of the covalent radii of the two atoms. CO has a triple bond so has minimum bond length.

questions.question: According to Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle, the product of the uncertainties in the position and momentum of an electron is always:
questions.question_html: According to Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle, the product of the uncertainties in the position and momentum of an electron is always:

options.text: greater than or equal to h/2 $\pi$
greater than or equal to h/3 $\pi$
options.html: greater than or equal to h/3{tex}\pi{/tex}

options.text: greater than or equal to h/ 6 $\pi$
options.html: greater than or equal to h/ 6{tex}\pi{/tex}

options.text: greater than or equal to h/4 $\pi$ .

correct_options: greater than or equal to h/4 $\pi$ .
questions.explanation:
The uncertainty principle says that we cannot measure the position (x) and the momentum (p) of a particle with absolute precision. The more accurately we know one of these values, the less accurately we know the other. Multiplying together the errors in the measurements of these values (the errors are represented by the triangle symbol in front of each property, the Greek letter "delta") has to give a number greater than or equal to half of a constant called "h-bar". This is equal to Planck's constant (usually written as h) divided by 2 $\pi$ .

 $Δx. Δp \ge { h\over 4\pi}$

questions.question_html: The increasing order of ionic radii for given species is:

options.html: {tex}Na^+ {<} \ F^- \ {<} \ O^{2-} \ {<} \ N^{3-} \ {<} \ Al^ {3+} \ {<} \ Mg^{2+}{/tex}
{tex} N^{3-} \ {<} \ O^{2-} \ {<} F^- \ {<} \ Na^+ \ {<} \ Mg^{2+} \ {<} \ Al^{3+}{/tex}

questions.explanation:
In the case of cations,
 
 there is a decrease in ionic radii of the resulting cation as compared to its neutral parent atom, due to loss of electron /(s)
 greater the positive charge over the cation more would be a decrease in ionic size or radius.
 
 
 In the case of anion,
 
 the addition of each new electron (or negative charge) results in an increase in anionic radius.
 With an increase in negative charge over anion the anionic radius also becomes larger.

 Thus, considering as above the correct answer is-

 $Al^{3+} \ {<} \ Mg^{2+} \ {<} \ Na^+ \ {<} \ F^- {<} O^{2-} \ {<} \ N^{3-}$

questions.explanation: An electron, due to its angular motion around the nucleus, generates an electric field. This electric field in turn produces a magnetic field that can interact with the external magnetic field. Thus, under the influence of the external magnetic field, the electrons of a subshell can orient themselves in certain preferred regions of space around the nucleus called orbitals. These orbitals of the same sub-shell having equal energy are called degenerate orbitals. In the presence of an external magnetic field, this degeneracy split, and the orbitals of the same subshell acquire slightly different energies. This causes the splitting of a given spectral line into many.

questions.question: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers represents the highest energy of an atom?
questions.question_html: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers represents the highest energy of an atom?
questions.explanation:
The respective orbitals are:

 3d, n+l = 5
 3p, n+l = 4
 4s, n+l = 4
 3s, n+ l = 3

The energies of orbitals increases with the increasing value of n+l according to Bohr' bury rule. So, set (n = 3, l = 2, m = 1, s = +1/ 2 ) of quantum numbers represent the highest energy.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
Chromatography is an important technique extensively used to separate mixtures into their components, purify compounds and also test the purity of compounds. Based on the principle involved, chromatography is classified into different categories. Two of these are Adsorption chromatography and Partition chromatography. Two main types of chromatographic techniques are based on the principle of differential adsorption column chromatography, and thin-layer chromatography. Adsorption chromatography is based on the fact that different compounds are adsorbed on an adsorbent to different degrees. Column chromatography involves the separation of a mixture over a column of adsorbent (stationary phase) packed in a glass tube. Thin-layer chromatography (TLC) is another type of adsorption chromatography, which involves the separation of substances of a mixture over a thin layer of an adsorbent coated on a glass plate. Partition chromatography is based on the continuous differential partitioning of components of a mixture between stationary and mobile phases.

Assertion (A): In adsorption chromatography, different compounds are adsorbed on an adsorbent to a different degree.

Reason (R): Commonly used adsorbents in chromatography are silica gel and alumina.

questions.question: The correct statement with regard to $H_2^ + $ and $H_2^ - $ is:
questions.question_html: The correct statement with regard to {tex}H_2^ + {/tex} and {tex}H_2^ - {/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Bond order of both species is $\frac {1}{2}$ but $H_2^ -$ ​ contains one electron in the antibonding orbital which results in repulsion and decreases the stability. On the other hand, $H_2^ + $ does not contain any electron in the antibonding molecular orbital.

Hence $H_2^ + $ ​ is more stable.

questions.question: Among the following, the maximum covalent character is shown by the compound is:
questions.question_html: Among the following, the maximum covalent character is shown by the compound is:
questions.explanation:
Few ionic bonds have partial covalent characteristics which were first discussed by a scientist, Kazimierz Fajans in 1923. He gave the rule to predict whether a chemical bond will be covalent or ionic which is now commonly known as Fajan’s rule. The rule can be stated as:

 Size of the ion: Smaller the size of cation, larger the size of anion, greater is the covalent character of the ionic bond.
 Charge of Cation: Greater the charge of cation, greater is the covalent character of the ionic bond.
 Electronic configuration- For cations with the same charge and size, the one, with (n-1)d^(n)ns^(​​​0) which is found in transition elements, has greater covalent character than the cation with ns^(2)np^(6) electronic configuration, which is commonly found in alkali or alkaline earth metals.

Keeping these rules in mind, we can say that AlCl_(​​​3) has a high covalent character.

questions.question:
The formation of sodium chloride involves two half-reactions, which clearly shows the involvement of electrons.

2Na (s) $\longrightarrow$ 2Na + (g) + 2e

Cl_(2 )(g) + 2e $\longrightarrow$ 2 Cl(g)

Sum of the half reactions gives

2 Na(s) + Cl_(2)(g) $\longrightarrow$ 2 Na + Cl (s) or 2 NaCl (s)

In terms of electron-transfer change, name the oxidising agent(s) and reducing agent(s).
questions.question_html:
The formation of sodium chloride involves two half-reactions, which clearly shows the involvement of electrons.
2Na (s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Na + (g) + 2e
Cl2 (g) + 2e  {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  2 Cl(g)
Sum of the half reactions gives
2 Na(s) + Cl2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  2 Na + Cl (s) or 2 NaCl (s)
In terms of electron-transfer change, name the oxidising agent(s) and reducing agent(s).

options.text: Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and oxidising agents: sodium and sulphur
options.html: Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and oxidising agents: sodium and sulphur

options.text: Oxidising agent: Chlorine and Reducing agent: Sodium
Oxidising agents: Chlorine, oxygen and reducing agents: sodium and sulphur
options.html: Oxidising agents: Chlorine, oxygen and reducing agents: sodium and sulphur

options.text: Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and sulphur and oxidising agent: sodium
options.html: Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and sulphur and oxidising agent: sodium

correct_options: Oxidising agent: Chlorine and Reducing agent: Sodium
questions.explanation:
According to the modern concept, the loss of electrons is oxidation whereas the gain of electrons is reduction. Redox reaction involves two half-reactions, one involving loss of electron or electrons (oxidation) and the other involving gain of electrons or electrons (reduction)

 Oxidizing agent is a species that can gain one or more electrons.
 Reducing agent is a species which can lose one or more electrons.

In the given reaction Sodium(Na) is losing 2 electrons, thus it is the reducing agent. Chlorine(Cl) is gaining 2 electrons, thus it is an oxidising agent.

questions.question: The density of 3 M solution of NaCl is 1.25gmL^(-1). The molality of the solution is:
questions.question_html: The density of 3 M solution of NaCl is 1.25gmL-1. The molality of the solution is:
questions.explanation:
Given: M = 3 mol L^(-1)

 $\therefore $ Mass of NaCl in 1 L of solution

= 3 $\times$ 58.5 = 175.5 g

 $\therefore $ Mass of 1L solution

= (1000 $\times$ 1.25) g = 1250 g

(since density = 1.25 g mL^(-1))

Mass of water in solution

= (1250 - 175.5) g = 1074.5 g

Since ,Molality 

= (No. of moles of solute / Mass of solvent in kgs)

 $\therefore$ Molality ( m) of the solution

= (3 mol / 1.0745 kg) = 2.79 m

questions.question: An element with mass number 81 contains 31.7 % more neutrons as compared to protons. Assign the atomic symbol.
questions.question_html: An element with mass number 81 contains 31.7 % more neutrons as compared to protons. Assign the atomic symbol.
questions.explanation:
P + N = 81

N = P + (0.317)P

Solving these two equations we get P = 35

The atomic number of Bromine is 35.

So the symbol is ${}_{35}^{81}{\text{Br}}$

questions.explanation:
Accuracy and precision are two important factors for consideration when taking data measurements.

ISO ( International Organisation for Standardisation) applies a rigid definition of ' Precision ' & ' Accuracy-

Precision is how consistent the results are when measurements are repeated.

Accuracy refers to how close a measurement is to the true value.

questions.explanation: Due to the presence of a double bond in the carbonyl carbon ( $>\mathrm C=\mathrm O$ ), carbon is sp^(2) hybridised. This hybridization leads to a trigonal planar geometry which means a bond angle of 120° around the C.

questions.question: How many molecules of H_(2)O are there in 18g of water? (Hint: Avogadro’s Number = 6.02 $\times$ 10^(23) atoms/mol)
questions.question_html: How many molecules of H2O are there in 18g of water? (Hint: Avogadro’s Number = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms/mol)
questions.explanation:
Since the molar mass of water

= 18 g / mol

 $ \therefore $ 1 mol of water (ie. 18 g ) would contain a number of molecules

= Avogadro No. (N)

= 6.02 $\times$ 10^(23)

* Note that Avogadro number (N) represents the number of entities (atoms, molecules, ions, or any other particle) constituting one mole of a particular substance.

questions.question: A co-ordinate bond is formed by:
questions.question_html: A co-ordinate bond is formed by:

options.text: Sharing of electrons contributed by both the atoms
options.html: Sharing of electrons contributed by both the atoms

options.text: Sharing of electrons contributed by one atom only
options.html: Sharing of electrons contributed by one atom only

correct_options: Sharing of electrons contributed by one atom only
questions.explanation:
Covalent bond is formed by two atoms sharing a pair of electrons. The atoms are held together because the electron pair is attracted by both of the nuclei.

In the formation of a simple covalent bond, each atom supplies one electron to the bond. But a co-ordinate bond (also called a dative covalent bond) is a covalent bond (a shared pair of electrons) in which both electrons come from the same atom.

questions.question: Geometrical isomers and Optical isomers are:
questions.question_html: Geometrical isomers and Optical isomers are:
questions.explanation: They are stereoisomers as they differ in the orientation of atoms in space.

questions.question: How many atoms of hydrogen are in 67.2 L of H_(2) at STP?
questions.question_html: How many atoms of hydrogen are in 67.2 L of H2 at STP?
questions.explanation:
One mole of any gas at STP has a volume of 22.4 L.

The number of moles = $\frac {67.2} {22.4}$ = 3 moles of H_(2)

One mole of any substance contains Avogadro's number of atoms = 6.022 $\times$ 10^(23) atoms.

So multiply the number of moles $\times$ number of atoms/mole = 1.8066 $\times$ 10^(24) atoms of H_(2).

questions.question: The product of the uncertainties of the exact position and exact momentum (or velocity) of an electron. The product of their uncertainties is always
questions.question_html: The product of the uncertainties of the exact position and exact momentum (or velocity) of an electron. The product of their uncertainties is always

options.text: equal to or greater than h/ $\pi$
options.html: equal to or greater than h/{tex}\pi{/tex}

options.text: equal to or greater than h/4 $\pi$ .
options.html: equal to or greater than h/4{tex}\pi{/tex}.

options.text: equal to or greater than h/3 $\pi$
options.html: equal to or greater than h/3{tex}\pi{/tex}

options.text: equal to or greater than h/2 $\pi$
options.html: equal to or greater than h/2{tex}\pi{/tex}

correct_options: equal to or greater than h/4 $\pi$ .
questions.explanation:
The uncertainty principle says that we cannot measure the position (x) and the momentum (p) of a particle with absolute precision. The more accurately we know one of these values, the less accurately we know the other. Multiplying together the errors in the measurements of these values has to give a number greater than or equal to half of a constant called "h-bar". This is equal to Planck's constant (usually written as h) divided by 2 $\pi$ .

 $Δx. Δp \ge { h\over 4\pi}$

questions.question: Calgon, which is used as a water softener, has the formula
questions.question_html: Calgon, which is used as a water softener, has the formula
questions.explanation:
Calgon is a trade name of a complex salt, sodium hexametaphosphate $Na_2[Na_4(PO_3)_6]$ . It is used for softening hard water. Calgon ionizes to give a complex anion:

 $\left(\mathrm{NaPO}_{3}\right)_6 \text { or } \mathrm{Na}_{2}\left(\mathrm{Na}_{4} \mathrm{P}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{18}\right) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\underset{\text { complex anion }}{\mathrm{Na} _4 \mathrm{P}_6 \mathrm{O}_{18}^{2-}}$ 

The addition of Calgon to hard water causes the calcium and magnesium ions of hard water to displace sodium ions from the anion of Calgon.

 $\mathop {C{a^{2 + }}}\limits_{{\text{from hard water}}} + \mathop {Na{P_6}O_{18}^{2 - }}\limits_{{\text{anion of calgon}}} \to 2N{a^ + } + \mathop {CaN{a_2}{P_6}O_{18}^{2 - }}\limits_{{\text{goes into solution}}} $ 

This results in the removal of calcium and magnesium ions from hard water in the form of a complex with Calgon. The water is softened and sodium ions are released into water.

questions.question: To separate mixtures into their components, and also to test the purity of compounds, the popularly used technique is:
questions.question_html: To separate mixtures into their components, and also to test the purity of compounds, the popularly used technique is:
questions.explanation: Chromatography is used for the purpose given. In this technique the mixture of substances is applied onto a stationary phase, which may be a solid or a liquid. A pure solvent, a mixture of solvents, or a gas is allowed to move slowly over the stationary phase. The components of the mixture get gradually separated from one another.

questions.question: In which bond of CH_(3)CH_(2)CH_(2)Br the inductive effect is expected to be the least?
questions.question_html: In which bond of CH3CH2CH2Br the inductive effect is expected to be the least?
questions.explanation: As the distance of the functional group increases its inductive effect decreases. Carbon-3 is farthest from Br. So in the bond between carbon-2 and carbon-3 the inductive effect is the least.

questions.explanation: Quantum mechanics, science dealing with the behaviour of matter and light on the atomic and subatomic scale. It attempts to describe and account for the properties of molecules and atoms and their constituents—electrons, protons, neutrons, and other more esoteric particles such as quarks and gluons.

questions.question: Hydrogen bonds are formed in many compounds e.g., H_(2)O, HF, NH_(3). The boiling point of such compounds depends to a large extent on the strength of the hydrogen bond and the number of hydrogen bonds. The correct decreasing order of the boiling points of the above compounds is:
questions.question_html: Hydrogen bonds are formed in many compounds e.g., H2O, HF, NH3. The boiling point of such compounds depends to a large extent on the strength of the hydrogen bond and the number of hydrogen bonds. The correct decreasing order of the boiling points of the above compounds is:
questions.explanation:
The smaller the size of the atom, the greater is the electronegativity, and hence stronger is the H−bonding. Thus, the order of strength of H-bonding is HF > H_(2)O > NH_(3). 

But each HF molecule is linked only to two other HF molecules while each H2​O molecule is linked to four other H2​O molecules through H−bonding.

Hence, the decreasing order of boiling points is H_(2)​O > HF > NH_(3).

questions.question: Syngas is a mixture of:
questions.question_html: Syngas is a mixture of:
questions.explanation: Synthesis gas or briefly, syngas, is a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen. Syngas can be produced from many sources, including natural gas, coal, biomass, or virtually any hydrocarbon feedstock, by reaction with steam or oxygen. Syngas is a crucial intermediate resource for the production of hydrogen, ammonia, methanol, and synthetic hydrocarbon fuels.

questions.question: The commonly occurring isotopes of carbon atoms are: ${_{\text{6}}^{{\text{12}}}}{\text{C }}{,_{\text{6}}}^{{\text{13}}}{\text{C }}{,_{\text{6}}}^{{\text{14}}}{\text{C}}$ . They contain:
questions.question_html: The commonly occurring isotopes of carbon atoms are: {tex}{_{\text{6}}^{{\text{12}}}}{\text{C }}{,_{\text{6}}}^{{\text{13}}}{\text{C }}{,_{\text{6}}}^{{\text{14}}}{\text{C}}{/tex}. They contain:
questions.explanation:
Isotopes are atoms with the same number of protons but that have a different number of neutrons.

The atomic number of carbon = 6.

So, the number of protons in each isotope = 6.

Number of neutrons in $^{12}_6C$ = 12-6= 6.

Number of neutrons in $^{13}_6C$ = 13-6= 7.

Number of neutrons in $^{14}_6C$ = 14-6= 8.

questions.explanation: The absorption spectrum is produced when white light is passed through a substance and transmitted light is analyzed by a spectrograph. The dark spaces correspond to the light radiation absorbed by the substance. And the emission spectrum is produced by analyzing the radiant energy emitted by an excited substance by a spectrograph. Thus, discontinuous spectra consisting of a series of sharp lines and separated by dark bands are obtained.

questions.question: _______ involves separation of a mixture over a column of adsorbent packed in a glass tube.
questions.question_html: _______ involves separation of a mixture over a column of adsorbent packed in a glass tube.
questions.explanation: Column chromatography involves the separation of a mixture over a column of adsorbent (stationary phase) packed in a glass tube. The column is fitted with a stopcock at its lower end. The mixture adsorbed on soluble adsorbent is placed on the top of the adsorbent column packed in a glass tube. An appropriate eluant which is a liquid or a mixture of liquids is allowed to flow down the column slowly. Depending upon the degree to which the compounds are adsorbed, complete separation takes place.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
A qualitative measure of the ability of an atom in a chemical compound to attract shared electrons to itself is called electronegativity. Unlike ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy, it is not a measurable quantity. However, a number of numerical al scales of electronegativity of elements viz., Pauling scale, Mulliken-Jaffe scale, Allred-Rochow scale have been developed. In 1922 assigned arbitrarily a value of 4.0 to fluorine, the elements considered to have the greatest ability to attract electrons. The electronegativity of any given element is not constant; it varies depending on the element to which it is bound. Though it is not a measurable quantity, it does provide a means, strong tendency to gain electrons.

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/imgur/1632228328-cdmarv.jpg) 

Therefore, electronegativity is directly related to the non-metallic properties of elements. It can be further extended to say that electronegativity is inversely related to the metallic properties of elements. Thus, the increase in electronegativities across a period is accompanied by an increase in non-metallic properties (or decrease in metallic properties) of elements

Increasing order of electro negativity is

questions.question: In which of the following options the order of arrangement does not agree with the variation of the property indicated against it?
questions.question_html: In which of the following options the order of arrangement does not agree with the variation of the property indicated against it?

options.text: B < C < N < O (increasing first ionization enthalpy)
options.html: B < C < N < O (increasing first ionization enthalpy)

options.text: Al^(3)+ < Mg^(2)+ < Na^(+) < F^(-) (increase ionic size)
options.html: Al<sup>3</sup>+ < Mg<sup>2</sup>+ < Na<sup>+</sup> < F<sup>-</sup> (increase ionic size)

options.text: I< Br < CI < F (increasing electron gain enthalpy)
options.html: I< Br < CI < F (increasing electron gain enthalpy)

options.text: Li < Na < K < Rb (increasing metallic radius)
options.html: Li < Na < K < Rb (increasing metallic radius)

correct_options: I< Br < CI < F (increasing electron gain enthalpy)
questions.explanation:
Electron gain enthalpy decreases from top to bottom in a group but due to small size of fluorine it has lower electron gain enthalpy than chlorine. Hence, the correct order is I< Br < F < Cl.

Also, ionization enthalpy increases on moving towards right along a period but due to extra stability of nitrogen due to half-filled outer orbital there is higher exchange energy hence, it achieves higher stability due to which it has higher ionization energy than oxygen. Therefore, the correct order is B < C < O < N.

tests.instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: Generally, the first ionization energy increases along a period including some exceptions. One which is NOT an exception is ______.
questions.question_html: Generally, the first ionization energy increases along a period including some exceptions. One which is NOT an exception is ______.
questions.explanation: The high ionization energy of B, N, and Mg is due to the presence of stable electronic configuration in these atoms as compared to the next element in the period. For example, Be (Is^(2) 2s^(2)) has completely filled orbitals as compared to B (1s^(2) 2s^(2) 2p^(1)) where there is one electron in p-orbital that can easily be lost. Therefore, B has lower ionization energy than Be.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the electron is incorrect?
questions.question_html: Which of the following statements about the electron is incorrect?

options.text: It is a basic constituent of all atoms.
options.html: It is a basic constituent of all atoms.

options.text: It is a constituent of cathode rays.
options.html: It is a constituent of cathode rays.

options.text: It is a negatively charged particle.
options.html: It is a negatively charged particle.

options.text: The mass of electron is equal to the mass of neutron.
options.html: The mass of electron is equal to the mass of neutron.

correct_options: The mass of electron is equal to the mass of neutron.
questions.explanation:
Mass of one electron= 9.1 $\times$ 10^(-31) kg

Mass of neutron =1.67 $\times$ 10^(-27) kg. Therefore, they are not equal.

questions.question: The increasing order of bond dissociation energies of the following species is_____.
questions.question_html: The increasing order of bond dissociation energies of the following species is_____.
questions.explanation:
As bond order increases bond dissociation energy also increases.

Thus N_(2)

 The triple bonded N_(2) with bond order = 3 has the highest bond dissociation energy.
 The doubly bonded O_(2) with bond order = 2 has lesser bond dissociation energy than N_(2)
 F_(2) and Cl_(2) both are single bonded having the same bond order = 1 

But, the electron-electron repulsive force (e-e repulsion) is maximum in the F_(2) molecule due to its small size. This accounts for the lower bond dissociation energy of F_(2 ) as compared to Cl_(2).

questions.question: The number of dots around the Lewis symbols for the elements represent:
questions.question_html: The number of dots around the Lewis symbols for the elements represent:

options.text: the number of valence electrons of the element
options.html: the number of valence electrons of the element

correct_options: the number of valence electrons of the element
questions.explanation:
Gilbert N. Lewis is widely known for his use of simple symbolic representations of elements that show valence electrons as dots.

The Lewis electron-dot symbols focus on the electrons in the highest principal energy level in the atom, the valence electrons.

After all, these are the electrons that participate in chemical reactions. Lewis electron-dot symbols work well for the representative elements.

questions.question: Which of the following is most powerful oxidizing agent in the following:
questions.question_html: Which of the following is most powerful oxidizing agent in the following:
questions.explanation: Ozone act as powerful oxidising agent because it can easily decompose to give an atom of nascent oxygen which is more reactive than O_(2).

questions.question: Ammonia (NH_(3)) is an example of:
questions.question_html: Ammonia (NH3) is an example of:
questions.explanation: Dihydrogen under certain reaction conditions combines with almost all elements except noble gases to form compounds called hydrides. Electron –rich hydrides, as the name suggests, are compounds of hydrogen with other elements that have excess electrons present as lone-pairs. For example, ammonia –NH_(3) is a hydride of nitrogen that has one lone pair, water (H_(2)O) is a hydride of oxygen that has two lone pairs, etc. As these are electron-rich species, they can donate electrons that is they are lewis bases.

questions.question: Electronegativity of carbon atoms depends upon their state of hybridisation. In which of the following compounds, the carbon marked with asterisk is most electronegative?
questions.question_html: Electronegativity of carbon atoms depends upon their state of hybridisation. In which of the following compounds, the carbon marked with asterisk is most electronegative?
questions.explanation: Greater is the s character of the hybridised orbital, greater is the electronegativity of the atom. In a triply bonded C, there is sp hybridisation. In sp hybridization, the atom has maximum s character (50%). So in this case the carbon with a triple bond has maximum electronegativity.

questions.question: For the reaction, 2 N_(2)H_(4) (l) + N_(2)O_(4 )(l) $\to $ 3N_(2)(g) + 4H_(2)O(l), if 160 g of N_(2)H_(4 )is mixed with 160 g N_(2)O_(4), the yield of H_(2)O is:
questions.question_html: For the reaction, 2 N2H4 (l) + N2O4 (l) {tex}\to {/tex} 3N2(g) + 4H2O(l), if 160 g of N2H4 is mixed with 160 g N2O4, the yield of H2O is:
questions.explanation:
Calculations:

Moles of N_(2)H_(4)

= $\frac{160}{32}$ 

= 5 moles 

Moles of N_(2)O_(4)

= $\frac{160}{92}$ 

= 1.739 moles

As per stoichiometry of the given reaction,

2 moles of N_(2)H_(4) require 1 mole of N_(2)O_(4),

 $\therefore$ 5 mole of N_(2)H_(4) will need 2.5 moles of N_(2)O_(4)

. But we have only 1.73 mole of N_(2)O_(4) so it would be consumed first.

Thus N_(2)O_(4) is the limiting reagent for this reaction.

Again, since 1 mole of N_(2)O_(4 )yields water

= 4 mole of water

 $ \therefore$ 1.73 mole of N_(2)O_(4) will yield water

= 6.92 moles

 $\equiv $ (6.92 $\times$ 18) g

= 124.56g of water

questions.question: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about −3.4 eV. What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state?
questions.question_html: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about −3.4 eV. What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state?
questions.explanation:
Total energy of the electron, E = −3.4 eV

Kinetic energy of the electron is equal to the negative of the total energy.

K = −E = − (− 3.4) = +3.4 eV

Hence, the kinetic energy of the electron in the given state is +3.4 eV.

questions.question: The elements present in an organic compound are converted from covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. This test is called:
questions.question_html: The elements present in an organic compound are converted from covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. This test is called:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus present in an organic compound are detected by “Lassaigne’s test”. The elements present in the compound are converted from the covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. Cyanide, sulphide and halide of sodium so formed on sodium fusion are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This extract is known as sodium fusion extract.

questions.explanation: Lattice enthalpy is simply the change in Enthalpy associated with the formation of one mole of an ionic compound from its oppositely charged ions in their standard states under standard conditions.

questions.question:
Which of the following are not redox reactions?

a) K_(4)[Fe(CN)_(6)] + H_(2)SO_(4) + H_(2)O $ \to $ K_(2)SO_(4) + CO + FeSO_(4) + (NH_(4))_(2)SO_(4).

b) $CuS{O_4} + N{H_3} \to [Cu{(N{H_3})_4}S{O_4}$ .

c)​​​​​​​ $Mg + {N_2} \to M{g_3}{N_2}$ .

d) ${I_2} + 3C{l_2} \to 2IC{l_3}$
questions.question_html:
Which of the following are not redox reactions?

a) K4[Fe(CN)6] + H2SO4 + H2O {tex} \to {/tex} K2SO4 + CO + FeSO4 + (NH4)2SO4.

b)  {tex}CuS{O_4} + N{H_3} \to [Cu{(N{H_3})_4}S{O_4}{/tex}.

c)​​​​​​​ {tex}Mg + {N_2} \to M{g_3}{N_2}{/tex}.

d) {tex}{I_2} + 3C{l_2} \to 2IC{l_3}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
a) K_(4)[Fe(CN)_(6)] + H_(2)SO_(4) + H_(2)O $ \to $ K_(2)SO_(4) + CO + FeSO_(4) + (NH_(4))_(2)SO_(4)

b) $CuS{O_4} + N{H_3} \to [Cu{(N{H_3})_4}S{O_4}$ 

Since, oxidation number of each element does not change in these reactions, so these are not redox reactions.

questions.question: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.question_html: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.explanation: The interaction of the electrons in a sigma bond (usually C–H or C–C) with an adjacent empty (or partially filled) non-bonding p-orbital, antibonding $\sigma$ or $\pi$ orbital or filled $\pi$ orbital, to give an extended molecular orbital that increases the stability of the system is Hyperconjugation.

questions.question: The number of radial nodes for 3p orbital is __________.
questions.question_html: The number of radial nodes for 3p orbital is __________.
questions.explanation:
Number of radial nodes = n−l−1

where n = principal quantum number, l = azimuthal quantum number

For 3p orbital, n=3 and l=1

 $\therefore$ The number of radial nodes for 3p orbital =3−1−1=3−2=1.

questions.question: Electrochemical processes for extraction of highly reactive metal and non-metal involves one of the following. Choose the most appropriate option.
questions.question_html: Electrochemical processes for extraction of highly reactive metal and non-metal involves one of the following. Choose the most appropriate option.
questions.explanation: The electrochemical process for extraction of metal or non-metal from its ore involves the redox reaction i.e. oxidation and reduction takes place simultaneously. For example, extraction of Na and Cl_(2 )from NaCl.

questions.question: The inertness of noble gas was observed to be due to their electronic configurations. Choose the most appropriate:
questions.question_html: The inertness of noble gas was observed to be due to their electronic configurations. Choose the most appropriate:

options.text: Atomic mass is low so they are stable.
options.html: Atomic mass is low so they are stable.

options.text: Noble gases form ions to complete their outermost orbitals.
options.html: Noble gases form ions to complete their outermost orbitals.

options.text: Total number of electrons in the outermost shell is duplets.
options.html: Total number of electrons in the outermost shell is duplets.

options.text: Outermost orbitals of the noble gases are fully filled.
options.html: Outermost orbitals of the noble gases are fully filled.

correct_options: Outermost orbitals of the noble gases are fully filled.
questions.explanation: The six noble gases (Helium, Neon, Argon, Krypton, Xenon, Radon) are found in group 18 of the periodic table. These elements were considered to be inert gases until the 1960s because their oxidation number of 0 prevents the noble gases from forming compounds readily. All noble gases have the maximum number of electrons possible in their outer shell (2 for Helium, 8 for all others), making them stable.

questions.question: Carius tube is used in the estimations of which one of the following?
questions.question_html: Carius tube is used in the estimations of which one of the following?
questions.explanation: It is used in the estimation of Halogen and Sulphur. For estimation of sulphur a known mass of an organic compound is heated in a hard glass tube called a Carius tube with sodium peroxide or fuming nitric acid. For estimation of halogen, a known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a Carius tube, in a furnace.

questions.question: Choose the most appropriate option to complete the statement - " Whenever there is a rearrangement of atoms which makes or breaks chemical bonds __________."
questions.question_html: Choose the most appropriate option to complete the statement - " Whenever there is a rearrangement of atoms which makes or breaks chemical bonds __________."
questions.explanation: The occurrence of a chemical reaction is always associated with the breaking and formation of bonds of the reactant and products along with rearrangement of their atoms, hence the statement at option 1 is most appropriate i.e. " Whenever there is a rearrangement of atoms which makes or breaks chemical bonds a chemical reaction takes place."

questions.question: The formation of the Cl_(2 )molecule can be understood in terms of the sharing of a pair of electrons between the two chlorine atoms, each chlorine atom contributing one electron to the shared pair. Choose the most appropriate name of the bond that is formed:
questions.question_html: The formation of the Cl2 molecule can be understood in terms of the sharing of a pair of electrons between the two chlorine atoms, each chlorine atom contributing one electron to the shared pair. Choose the most appropriate name of the bond that is formed:
questions.explanation: Two chlorine atoms will each share one electron to get a full outer shell and form a stable Cl_(2) molecule. By sharing the two electrons where the shells touch each chlorine atom can count 8 electrons in its outer shell. These full outer shells with their shared electrons are now stable and the Cl_(2) molecule will not react further with other chlorine atoms. One pair of shared electrons form a single covalent bond.

questions.question: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.question_html: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.explanation: Catenation is the linkage of atoms of the same element into longer chains. Catenation occurs most readily in carbon, which forms covalent bonds with other carbon atoms to form longer chains and structures. This is the reason for the presence of the vast number of organic compounds in nature.

questions.question: Which of the following reactions is not correct according to the law of conservation of mass.
questions.question_html: Which of the following reactions is not correct according to the law of conservation of mass.
questions.explanation:
According to law of conservation of mass, the number of atoms in each side of reaction must be equal. i.e mass of reactant = mass of product.

In this equation, No. of atoms in the reactant side is not equal to the no. of atoms in the product side.

Hence, law of conservation of mass is not satisfied.

questions.explanation: The electron microscope uses a beam of accelerated electrons as a source of illumination. The wavelength of an electron being much shorter than the visible light the electron microscope has a higher resolving power.

questions.explanation: Bond length is inversely proportional to bond order. $O_2^{2 + }$ has highest bond order as electrons are removed from antibonding MO.

questions.question: The ionic hydrides are crystalline, non-volatile but on melting does one of the following:
questions.question_html: The ionic hydrides are crystalline, non-volatile but on melting does one of the following:
questions.explanation: They are formed when hydrogen molecule reacts with most s-block elements which are highly electropositive in nature. In solid-state, the ionic hydrides are crystalline, non-conducting, and non-volatile. However, in a liquid state, it conducts electricity. Ionic hydrides on electrolysis liberate hydrogen gas at the anode.

questions.question: Polarisation of $\sigma$ -bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent $\sigma$ -bond is termed as:
questions.question_html: Polarisation of {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond is termed as:
questions.explanation: Polarisation of $\sigma$ -bond caused by the polarisation of the adjacent $\sigma$ -bond is referred to as the inductive effect. This effect is passed on to the subsequent bonds also but the effect decreases rapidly as the number of intervening bonds increases and becomes vanishingly small after three bonds. The inductive effect is related to the ability of substituent(s) to either withdraw or donate electron density to the attached carbon atom.

questions.question: Mg^(2+) is isoelectronic with:
questions.question_html: Mg2+ is isoelectronic with:
questions.explanation:
Isoelectronic species refers to the elements that have the same number of electrons.

Mg^(2+) is 10 electron species $(1s^2, 2s^2, 2p^6)$ . Its configuration is like that of Ne.

Thus it is isoelectronic with any element having 10e^(-) or we can say 8e^(- )in its valence shell.

Atomic number of Sodium (Na) is 11 after loosing one electron it became Na^(+) and have 10 electrons.

Thus Mg^(2+) is isoelectronic with Na^(+).
