questions.question_html: Dihydrogen can be prepared on a commercial scale by different methods. In its preparation by the action of steam on hydrocarbons, a mixture of CO and H2 gas is formed. It is known as ________.
questions.explanation: Water gas is a combustion fuel containing carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen gas (H_(2)). Water-gas is made by passing steam overheated hydrocarbons. The water-gas shift reaction can be used to reduce carbon dioxide levels and enrich hydrogen content, making water gas.

questions.question: Inorganic compounds, when homolytic cleavage takes place, one of the electrons of the shared pair in a covalent bond goes with each of the bonded atoms. This results in the formation of:
questions.question_html: Inorganic compounds, when homolytic cleavage takes place, one of the electrons of the shared pair in a covalent bond goes with each of the bonded atoms. This results in the formation of:
questions.explanation: In homolytic cleavage or homolysis, the two electrons in a cleaved covalent bond are divided equally between the products. Thus, in homolytic cleavage, the movement of a single electron takes place instead of an electron pair. This process is also known as homolytic fission. Such cleavage results in the formation of neutral species (atom or group) which contains an unpaired electron. These species are called free radicals.

questions.question: The number of grams of oxygen in 0.10 mol of Na_(2)CO_(3). 10H_(2)O is
questions.question_html: The number of grams of oxygen in 0.10 mol of Na2CO3. 10H2O is
questions.explanation:
Calculations:

1 mole of Na_(2)CO_(3).10H_(2)O contains 13 moles of oxygen atoms. 

0.1 mole Na_(2)CO_(3).10H_(2)O contains 13 × 0.1 = 1.3 moles of oxygen atoms. 

Mass of oxygen atoms = 1.3 × 16 = 20.8 g.

 $\therefore$ 0.10 mole of Na_(2 )CO_(3). 10 H_(2 )O would contain a number of grams of oxygen atoms = 20.8 g

questions.question: Charge on the electron was experimentally determined by:
questions.question_html: Charge on the electron was experimentally determined by:

options.text: Electrical discharge carried out in the modified cathode ray tube
options.html: Electrical discharge carried out in the modified cathode ray tube
R.A. Millikan's oil drop experiment

questions.explanation: In this experiment, a spray of oil droplets produced by an atomizer is allowed to fall in between two oppositely charged plates. These atoms are converted into ions with the help of X-rays. The oil droplets may absorb some of the electrons thus produced and acquire charge. The velocity of the charged oil droplet is measured as it falls freely under the influence of gravity and then in an electric field. From the values of velocity measured, it is possible to calculate the charge on the droplet which is considered to be the charge of the electron.

questions.question: Considering x-axis as the internuclear axis which out of the following will not from a sigma bond and why?
questions.question_html: Considering x-axis as the internuclear axis which out of the following will not from a sigma bond and why?
questions.explanation:
For p-orbitals (l = 1), there are three possible orientations corresponding to m = -1, 0, +1 values. This means that there are three p - orbitals in each p-subshell. These are designated as p_(x), p_(y) and p_(z);

These three orbitals are equal in energy but differ in their orientations. Each orbital consists of two lobes symmetrical about a particular axis. Depending upon the orientation of the lobes, these are designated as 2p_(x), 2p_(y) and 2p_(z), as they are symmetrical about x, y and z-axes respectively. That is, 2p_(x) orbital has two lobes symmetrical around x-axis and the lobes of 2p_(z) orbital are symmetrical around z-axis while 2p_(y) orbital has two lobes symmetrical around y-axix so it doesn't form sigma bond.

questions.explanation:
Molecular mass is the sum of atomic masses of the elements present in a molecule.

For example ; Molecular mass of CO_(2 )= $\Sigma$ atomic mass of one carbon atom & atomic mass of two oxygen atoms

= [ 12 + 2(16) ]u

= 44u.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
A quantitative measure of the tendency of an element to lose electrons is given by its Ionization Enthalpy. It represents the energy required to remove an electron from an isolated gaseous atom (X) in its ground state. The ionization enthalpy is expressed in units of kJ mol^(-1). We can define the second ionization enthalpy as the energy required to remove the second most loosely bound electron. Energy is always required to remove electrons from an atom and hence ionization enthalpies are always positive. The second ionization enthalpy will be higher than the first ionization enthalpy.

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/imgur/1632228324-zzkbgx.jpg) 

Variation of first ionization enthalpies ( $\Delta_i$ H) with atomic number for elements with Z = 1 to 60. On the other hand, minima occur at the alkali metals and their low ionization enthalpies can be correlated with their high reactivity. In addition, you will notice two trends the first ionization enthalpy generally increases as we go across a period and decreases as we descend in a group. The ionization enthalpy and atomic radius are closely related properties.

The second ionization enthalpy will be higher than the first ionization enthalpy because
questions.question_html:

We can define the second ionization enthalpy as the energy required to remove the second most loosely bound electron. Energy is always required to remove electrons from an atom and hence ionization enthalpies are always positive. The second ionization enthalpy will be higher than the first ionization enthalpy.

Variation of first ionization enthalpies ({tex}\Delta_i{/tex}H) with atomic number for elements with Z = 1 to 60. On the other hand, minima occur at the alkali metals and their low ionization enthalpies can be correlated with their high reactivity. In addition, you will notice two trends the first ionization enthalpy generally increases as we go across a period and decreases as we descend in a group. The ionization enthalpy and atomic radius are closely related properties.


The second ionization enthalpy will be higher than the first ionization enthalpy because

tests.instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: Give the name and an atomic number of the inert gas atom in which the total number of d-electrons is equal to the difference between the numbers of total p and total s electrons.
questions.question_html: Give the name and an atomic number of the inert gas atom in which the total number of d-electrons is equal to the difference between the numbers of total p and total s electrons.
questions.explanation:
Kr has atomic no. 36. And in Kr total number of d-electrons is equal to the difference between the numbers of total p and total s electrons.

1s^(2),2s^(2),2p^(6),3s^(2),3p^(6),3d^(10),4s^(2),4p^(6)

Total s-electrons =8

Total p-electrons =18

Difference =18−8=10 

Total d-electrons =10

questions.question: Among the following pairs of orbitals which orbital will experience the larger effective nuclear charge? (i) 2s and 3s, (ii) 4d and 4f, (iii) 3d and 3p:
questions.question_html: Among the following pairs of orbitals which orbital will experience the larger effective nuclear charge? (i) 2s and 3s, (ii) 4d and 4f, (iii) 3d and 3p:
questions.explanation:
Nuclear charge is defined as the net positive charge experienced by an electron in the orbital of a multi-electron atom. The closer the orbital, the greater is the nuclear charge experienced by the electron (s) in it.

(i) 2s is closer to the nucleus than 3s.Hence 2s will experience larger effective nuclear charge.

(ii) 4d will experience greater nuclear charge than 4f since 4d is closer to the nucleus than 4f.

(iii) 3p will experience greater nuclear charge since it is closer to the nucleus than 3f because 3p is closer to nucleus than 3f.

questions.question: Self-ionization of water is called as ________.
questions.question_html: Self-ionization of water is called as ________.
questions.explanation:
In auto-protolysis a proton is transferred between two identical molecules, one of which acts as a Bronsted acid, releasing a proton that is accepted by the other molecule acting as a Bronsted base.

Auto-protolysis is also called the self-ionization. Auto-protolysis of water is a reaction in which the self-ionization of water molecules takes place to produce hydronium ion (H_(3)O^(+)) and hydroxide ion (OH^(​​​​​-)).

 $2H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_3O^++OH^-$

questions.question: Around 10^(15) Hz corresponds to the region of the electromagnetic spectrum
questions.question_html: Around 1015 Hz corresponds to the region of the electromagnetic spectrum
questions.explanation: A frequency of $10^{15} {~Hz}$ primarily corresponds to the ultraviolet (UV) region of the electromagnetic spectrum. The UV region spans frequencies between $10^{14} {~Hz}$ and $10^{17} {~Hz}$ . While visible light falls within $4 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}$ to $7.5 \times$ $10^{14} {~Hz}$ , a frequency around $10^{15} {~Hz}$ is near the upper limit of visible light and is generally considered to be part of the UV spectrum. Thus, $10^{15} {~Hz}$ most accurately belongs to the ultraviolet region.

questions.question: The oxidation number of carbon is zero in:
questions.question_html: The oxidation number of carbon is zero in:
questions.explanation:
In the given options following calculations occurs according to rules:

 HCHO: Since there are 2H atoms, +1 for each = +2, 1 O atom, -2 for each = -2 So since that already adds up to 0, carbon will have an oxidation number of 0.
 CH_(2)Cl_(2): Since there are 2H atoms, +1 for each = +2, 2 Cl atom, -1 for each = -2 So since that already adds up to 0, carbon will have an oxidation number of 0.
 C_(12)H_(22)O_(11): Since there are 22H atoms, +1 for each = +22, 11 O atom, -1 for each = -22 So since that already adds up to 0, carbon will have an oxidation number of 0.

questions.explanation: The given equation gets balanced by inserting the coefficients (3,1,1,3) in blank spaces from LHS to RHS. Thus, the balanced equation for the given reaction is 3KOH + H_(3)PO_(4) $\longrightarrow$ K_(3)PO_(4) + 3H_(2)O.

questions.question: The reduction in the attractive interaction of an electron in the outer shell and the nucleus due to the presence of electrons in the inner shells is known as _________.
questions.question_html: The reduction in the attractive interaction of an electron in the outer shell and the nucleus due to the presence of electrons in the inner shells is known as _________.
questions.explanation: The effective nuclear charge experienced by an electron depends upon the shell and the orbital in which the electron is present. The electrons in the outer shell are shielded from the nucleus by the electrons in the inner shells.

questions.question: Out of the following, intramolecular hydrogen bonding exists in.
questions.question_html: Out of the following, intramolecular hydrogen bonding exists in.
questions.explanation:
If a hydrogen bond occurs within two functional group of the same molecule, it is called an intramolecular hydrogen bond. This occurs when the hydrogen donor and the acceptor are both within the same molecule.

In the 2-nitro phenol molecule, the O atom in the –OH group is more electronegative than the H and hence δ-. H atom, on the other hand, is δ+. Therefore, the O atom in the –OH group acts as the H donor whereas O atom on the nitro group acts as the H-acceptor.

questions.question: Atoms like N, O, and F in hydrides have higher boiling points than those of the subsequent group member hydrides. It is because of:
questions.question_html: Atoms like N, O, and F in hydrides have higher boiling points than those of the subsequent group member hydrides. It is because of:

options.text: Higher electropositivity of N, O and F
options.html: Higher electropositivity of N, O and F

options.text: Shared electron pairs of N, O and F
options.html: Shared electron pairs of N, O and F

options.text: The magnitude of covalent bonding in their hydrides
options.html: The magnitude of covalent bonding in their hydrides

options.text: Higher electronegativity of N, O and F
options.html: Higher electronegativity of N, O and F

correct_options: Higher electronegativity of N, O and F
questions.explanation: The hydrides of N, F, and O as NH_(3), HF, and H_(​2)O have much higher boiling points than the subsequent group element hydrides. Actually, on the basis of small ​molar masses, these hydrides can be expected to have lower boiling points than the subsequent group element hydrides like PH_(3), HCl, or H_(2)S respectively but due to higher electronegativity the N, F, and O - atoms undergo extensive intermolecular H-bonding which is very strong and as a result NH_(3), HF, and H​_(2)O have higher boiling points in their subsequent group members.

questions.question: In Carius method of estimation of halogen, 0.15 g of an organic compound gave 0.12 g of AgBr. Find out the percentage of bromine in the compound.
questions.question_html: In Carius method of estimation of halogen, 0.15 g of an organic compound gave 0.12 g of AgBr. Find out the percentage of bromine in the compound.
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of AgBr = 108 + 80 = 188 g/mol

188 g AgBr contains 80 g bromine

0.12 g AgBr contains (80 $\times$ 0.12)/188 g bromine

Percentage of bromine $ = \frac{{(80 \times 0.12 \times 100)}}{{(188 \times 0.15)}} = 34.04\% $

questions.question: A known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate. This is
questions.question_html: A known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate. This is
questions.explanation: The Carius method in analytical chemistry is a method for the quantitative determination of halogens in organic substances. It involves heating a known mass of an organic compound with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a hard glass tube known as carius tube, in a furnace. The halogen present forms the corresponding silver halide (AgX). It is filtered, washed, dried, and weighed.

questions.question: The product of the magnitude of the charge and the distance between the centres of positive and negative charge is called _______.
questions.question_html: The product of the magnitude of the charge and the distance between the centres of positive and negative charge is called _______.
questions.explanation: A dipole moment ​is a measurement of the separation of two opposite electrical charges. Dipole moments are a vector quantity. The magnitude is equal to the charge multiplied by the distance between the charges and the direction is from negative charge to positive charge: $\mu$ = q $\times$ r

questions.question: In winter season ice formed on the surface of a lake. Why?
questions.question_html: In winter season ice formed on the surface of a lake. Why?

options.text: Lowers the temperature of the surrounding atmosphere
options.html: Lowers the temperature of the surrounding atmosphere

options.text: Kills the aquatic life in the lake
options.html: Kills the aquatic life in the lake

questions.explanation: Since there is no heat flux,​ ice once formed grows quickly on the surface of the lake. As this cover completely on the surface, the snow on the ice insulates the water from the atmosphere. Due to this, the rate of ice growth is slowed down. The heat lost from the water to the atmosphere must be taken from the latent heat released when ice is formed since the water just below the ice is at the freezing point. Due to this thermal insulation, water at the bottom of the lake does not freeze or the thickness of ice decreases on moving down the lake. Thus the aquatic animals and plants survive.

questions.question: When an organic compound is present in an aqueous medium, it is separated by:
questions.question_html: When an organic compound is present in an aqueous medium, it is separated by:
questions.explanation: Differential extraction or solvent extraction is an experimental technique where an organic compound can be isolated from its aqueous solution by shaking it with an organic solvent. The organic compound will dissolve in the organic solvent, and with the use of a separating funnel, the organic solvent and water mixture is separated. The organic compound is obtained by evaporating the organic solvent.

questions.question: 0.05 ml is equal to_______ litres?
questions.question_html: 0.05 ml is equal to_______ litres?
questions.explanation:
Since 1000 ml = 1 litre

 $\therefore$ 0.05 ml = (0.05 / 1000) litres

= 0.00005 litres

Thus, 0.05ml is equal to 0.00005 litres.

questions.explanation:
Exceptional electronic configuration arises because of the extra stability due to the half-filled and fully filled electronic configuration. Half filled and fully filled electronic configuration has extra stability due to two factors:

 Symmetrical arrangement.
 Stability due to the exchange energy.

questions.question: The principle involved in paper chromatography is
questions.question_html: The principle involved in paper chromatography is
questions.explanation: Paper chromatography is a type of partition chromatography. In paper chromatography, a special quality paper known as chromatography paper is used. Chromatography paper contains water trapped in it, which acts as the stationary phase. Separation of components of a mixture depends upon their partitioning between water held in stationary phase (adsorbant paper) and the liquid present in the mobile phase.

questions.question: On complete combustion, 0.246 g of an organic compound gave 0.198g of carbon dioxide and 0.1014g of water. Determine the percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in the compound.
questions.question_html: On complete combustion, 0.246 g of an organic compound gave 0.198g of carbon dioxide and 0.1014g of water. Determine the percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in the compound.
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of carbondioxide=44 g/mol

Molar mass of C=12 g/mol

44 g CO_(​​​​​2 )contains 12 g C

0.198 g of CO_(​​​​​2 )contains (12 x 0.198 g)/44 C

% of C= (12x0.198x100)/(44x0.246)=21.95%

Molar mass of water=18 g/mol

18 g water contains 2 g H

0.1014 g water contains (2x0.1014 g)/18H

% of H= (2 x 0.1014 x 100)/(0.246 x 18)=4.58 %

questions.question: Electronic configuration of an element having atomic number 24.
questions.question_html: Electronic configuration of an element having atomic number 24.
questions.explanation:
![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/cbse/rvw/ques_21296.png) 

The 'Aufbau' principle (represented in the diagram above) can be used to determine the electron structure of relatively simple atoms, by hand, as long as one remembers the anomalies (such as chromium and copper). The 'anomaly' is typically 'explained' by stating that the a 'half-filled' or 'completely filled' d-subshell is more stable and will, therefore, happen before 'completely filling' the 4s subshell. So to have more stability 4s and 3d orbital are half filled instead of 2 electrons in 4s and 4 electrons in 3d orbitals.

questions.question: The molecular formula C_(3)H_(8)O represents two alcohols: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol. This property is called as:
questions.question_html: The molecular formula C3H8O represents two alcohols: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol. This property is called as:
questions.explanation: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol are positional isomers as they differ only in the position of functional group -OH. Positional isomers are isomers that have the same carbon skeleton and the same functional groups but differ from each other in the location of the functional groups on or in the carbon chain.

questions.question: Bohr’s model of atom explains the spectrum of:
questions.question_html: Bohr’s model of atom explains the spectrum of:
questions.explanation:
The emission spectrum of atomic hydrogen is divided into a number of spectral series, with wavelengths given by the Rydberg formula. These observed spectral lines are due to the electron making transitions between two energy levels in the atom.

Bohr tells us that the electrons in the Hydrogen atom can only occupy discrete orbits around the nucleus (not at any distance from it but at certain specific, quantized, positions, or radial distances each one corresponding to an energetic state of your H atom) where they do not radiate energy.

When the electron moves from one allowed orbit to another it emits or absorbs photons of energy matching exactly the separation between the energies of the given orbits (emission/absorption spectrum).

questions.explanation: There are 8 protons in a single atom of oxygen and 8 neutrons. Thus, a molecule of oxygen (O_(​​​​​​2)) would contain 16 of each. To gain + charge it must have to loose one electron. So number of electrons in $O_2^+$ = 16-1=15.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
The period indicates the value of n for the outermost or valence shell. In other words, a successive period in the Periodic Table is associated with the filling of the next higher principal energy level (n = 1, n = 2, etc.). Elements in the same vertical column or group have similar valence shell electronic configurations, the same number of electrons in the outer orbitals, and similar properties. Atomic Radius refers to both covalent or metallic radius depending on whether the element is a non-metal or a metal. Atomic radius. The elements are classified into four blocks viz., s-block, p-block, d-block, and f-block depending on the type of atomic orbitals that are being filled with electron. helium belongs to the s-block but its positioning in the p-block along with another group of 18 elements is justified because it has a completely filled valence shell (1s^(2)) and as a result, exhibits properties characteristic of other noble gases.

questions.question: Among the following elements, which has the least electron affinity?

questions.explanation: Nitrogen has electronic configuration of 1s^(2) 2s^(2) 2p^(3). It has half-filled p orbitals in this outermost shell which is a stable configuration. It is difficult to add electron in such orbitals and lesser energy is released on the addition of electrons hence the electron affinity value will decrease.

questions.question_html: BaCl2.2H2O is an example of:
questions.explanation:
Water present in the interstitial sites in a crystal lattice is known as interstitial water. They are not held by hydrogen bonding with the other elements. If they are bonded they are called H-bonded hydrates.

Barium chloride is a white crystalline powder. It contains two molecules of water. It is one of the most important water-soluble salts of Barium.

questions.question: The large number of organic compounds is due to
questions.question_html: The large number of organic compounds is due to

options.text: both catenation and the tetravalency of carbon
options.html: both catenation and the tetravalency of carbon

correct_options: both catenation and the tetravalency of carbon
questions.explanation: The element carbon has the unique property called catenation (self-linkage) due to which it forms covalent bonds with other carbon atoms. It also forms covalent bonds with atoms of other elements like hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur, phosphorus and halogens. It has a valency four because of which it can form four bonds. Because of the above two properties, it can exist in straight chain, branched chain or cyclic forms. It can form single, double a well as triple bonds and hence it can form a large number and variety of compounds.

questions.question: Calgon is used in:
questions.question_html: Calgon is used in:
questions.explanation: Calgon is a process of removing the permanent hardness of the water. Calgon is a complex salt, Sodium hexametaphosphate Na(PO_(3))_(6), or Na_(6)P_(6)O_(18). It acts as an ion exchange resin. It is used for the softening of water. When hard water containing Ca and Mg ions are reacted with Calgon, it displaces these ions with Sodium-ion, by forming a complex with itself and thus the water becomes free of these ions which cause hardness.

questions.explanation: Scientific notation is conveniently used to represent extremely large or even very small numerical values. It is an exponential notation in which any number can be represented in the form, N $\times$ 10^(n), where n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary between 1 and 10, but not 10 itself.

questions.question: Dalton's atomic theory could not explain one of the following
questions.question_html: Dalton's atomic theory could not explain one of the following
questions.explanation:
Dalton’s atomic theory suffered from the following drawbacks:

 Atoms of the same or different types have a strong tendency to combine together to form a new ‘group of atoms’. For example, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen gases exist in nature as ‘group of two atoms’. This indicates that the smallest unit capable of independent existence is not an atom, but a ‘group of atoms’.
 With the discovery of sub-atomic particles, e.g., electrons, neutrons, and protons, the atom can no longer be considered indivisible.
 Discovery of isotopes indicated that all atoms of the same element are not perfectly identical. At least, they differ in their masses. Atoms of the same element having different masses are called isotopes.

questions.question:
What is the correct order of decreasing stability of the following cations?

 $\underset{\text{}}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-C{{H}_{3}}}}\,$ 
 $\underset{}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-OC{{H}_{3}}}}\,$ 
 $\underset{}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-C{{H}_{2}}-OC{{H}_{3}}}}\,$
questions.question_html:
What is the correct order of decreasing stability of the following cations?

  1. {tex}\underset{\text{}}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-C{{H}_{3}}}}\,{/tex}
  2. {tex}\underset{}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-OC{{H}_{3}}}}\,{/tex}
  3. {tex}\underset{}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-C{{H}_{2}}-OC{{H}_{3}}}}\,{/tex}
questions.explanation:
In (I) case, + vely charged C is attached to two alkyl groups and +I effect stabilizes carbocation.
 In (II) case, stable due to +R effect of -OCH_(3) group stabilizes carbocation.
 In (III) case, -I effect of -OCH_(3) group. destabilizes carbocation. hence, the order of stability: II > I > III.

questions.question: During estimation of nitrogen present in an organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method, the ammonia evolved from 0.5 g of the compound, neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H_(2)SO_(4.) Find out the percentage of nitrogen in the compound.
questions.question_html: During estimation of nitrogen present in an organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method, the ammonia evolved from 0.5 g of the compound, neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H2SO4. Find out the percentage of nitrogen in the compound.
questions.explanation:
1 M of 10 mL H_(2)SO_(4)= 1 M of 20 mL NH_(3)

1000 mL of 1 M ammonia contains 14 g nitrogen

20 mL of 1 M ammonia contains $\frac{{(14 \times 20g)}}{{1000}}$ nitrogen

% of nitrogen = $ \frac{{(14 \times 20 \times 100)}}{{1000 \times 0.5}}$ = 56.0%

questions.question: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 $\times$ 10^(15) Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 $\times$ 10^(-34)Js).
questions.question_html: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34Js).
questions.explanation:
We know Planck's equation is $E= h\nu$ 

where E is energy, h is Planck’s constant and $\nu $ is frequency.

Put the given values,

E = $6.626\times 10^{-34}\times 3\times 10^{15}$ $= 1.988\times 10^{-18}J$

questions.question: Which of the following has maximum number of lone pairs associated with Xe?
questions.question_html: Which of the following has maximum number of lone pairs associated with Xe?
questions.explanation:
In XeF_(​​​​2): Xe has 2 of its electrons in bonding with F. So, since Xe has 8 valence electrons, the no.of lone pairs = ${no. \ of \ non-bonding \ electrons\over 2}={6\over 2}=3$ 
 In XeO_(3): Xe has 6 of its electrons in bonding with O (bcoz of double bond). So, Xe will have 2 non bonding electrons and therefore 1 lone pair.
 In XeF_(4): Xe has 4 of its electrons in bonding with F. So, Xe will have 4 non bonding electrons and therefore 2 lone pairs.
 In XeF_(6): Xe has 6 of its electrons in bonding with F. So, Xe will have 2 non bonding electrons and therefore 1 lone pair.

So XeF_(2) has the maximum no. of lone pairs associated with Xe.

options.text: - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_(2), > C = C <, - C $\equiv$ C -, -CONH_(2)
options.html: - CN, - HC = O, > C = O,  - OH, - NH2, > C = C <, - C {tex}\equiv{/tex} C -, -CONH2

options.html: - HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH2, > C = C <, - C {tex}\equiv {/tex}C -, -CONH2, - CN

options.html: - C = O, - OH, - NH2, > C = C <, - C {tex}\equiv{/tex} C -, -CONH2, - CN > - HC = O

options.text: -CONH_(2),-CN, -HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_(2),> C = C <, -C $\equiv$ C-

questions.explanation:
The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:

-CONH_(2), -CN, -HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_(2), > C = C <, -C $\equiv$ C-

Carboxlic acid > sulphonic acid > acid anhydride > ester > acyl halide > acyl amide > nitrile > aldehyde > ketones > alcohol > amine > alkene > alkyne.

questions.question:
Consider the reaction,

 $2{C_4}{H_{10}}(g) + 13{O_2}(g) \to 8C{O_2}(g) + 10{H_2}O(l)$ 

If 300g of C_(4)H_(10) (g) is burnt with 1000 g of O_(2), the yield of H_(2)O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are _____ and _____ respectively.
questions.question_html:
Consider the  reaction,
{tex}2{C_4}{H_{10}}(g) + 13{O_2}(g) \to 8C{O_2}(g) + 10{H_2}O(l){/tex}
If 300g of C4H10 (g) is burnt with 1000 g of O2, the yield of H2O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are _____ and _____ respectively.
questions.explanation:
Calculations

As per the stoichiometry of the given equation,

 $2{C_4}{H_{10}}(g) + 13{O_2}(g) \to 8C{O_2}(g) + 10{H_2}O(l)$ 

Calculations:

Step 1/ -

Molar mass of C_(4)H_(10)

= (4 $\times$ 2 + 1 $\times$ 10) amu = (48 + 10) amu

= 58 amu

 $\therefore$ 300g of C_(4)H_(10)

= $\frac{300}{58}$ moles

= 5.17 moles

Step 2 / -

 For the yield of H_(2)O;

 Since 2 mols of C_(4)H_(10) yields 10 H_(2)O i.e.

 = 10 moles.

 $\therefore$ 5.17 moles of C_(4)H_(10) would yield 10H_(2)O_( )

 $ = \frac{{5.17 \times 10}}{2}moles$ 

 = 25.85 moles

 $ = (25.85 \times 18)g$ 

 465.3 g $\approx$ 465 g
 For predicting the limiting reagent;
 
 Calculate the number of moles of O_(2) needed to react with 5.17 moles of C_(4)H_(10)

 Since 2 moles of C_(4)H_(10) requires 13 O_(2) molecules i.e.

 = 13 moles

 $ = \frac{{13 \times 5.17}}{2}moles$ 

 = 33.62 moles

 Number of moles of O_(2 )actually taken for combustion

 = $\frac{mass \ of \ oxygen \ taken }{ molar \ mass \ of \ oxygen}$ 

 = $\frac{1000}{32} moles$ 

 = 31.25 moles

 Thus, it is noteworthy that,

 O_(2(g)) taken for reaction (i.e. 31.25 moles) is lesser than the required number of moles (i.e. 33.62 moles).

 Hence, it would be consumed first and is, therefore, the limiting reagent for this reaction.

questions.question: How many neutrons and protons are there in the $^{13}_6C$ nucleus?
questions.question_html: How many neutrons and protons are there in the  {tex}^{13}_6C{/tex} nucleus?
questions.explanation:
Number of protons = Atomic number =6

Number of neutrons = atomic mass - no of protons = 13-6 = 7

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
Every experimental measurement has some amount of uncertainty associated with it because of the limitation of the measuring instrument and the skill of the person making the measurement. Significant figures are meaningful digits that are known with certainty plus one which is estimated or uncertain. The uncertainty is Density involved mass and volume. The density of a substance is its amount of mass per unit volume. The density of a substance tells us about how closely its particles are packed. If the density is more, it means particles are more closely packed. Temperature are three common scales to measure temperature — °C (degree celsius), Fahrenheit) and K (kelvin). Here, K is the SI unit. The thermometers are based on these scales indicate by writing the certain digits and the last uncertain digit. Precision refers to the closeness of various measurements for the same quantity. However, accuracy is the agreement of a particular value to the true value of the result. 

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/imgur/1632228481-zgyzfb.jpg) 

The significant figures in 3400 are

tests.instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: The elements with atomic numbers 35, 53 and 85 are all ________.
questions.question_html: The elements with atomic numbers 35, 53 and 85 are all ________.
questions.explanation: Halogens have atomic numbers 9, 17, 35, 53, and 85. The element are fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine, and astatine.

questions.question: An electron beam undergoes diffraction. This wave character of electrons is used in the making of:
questions.question_html: An electron beam undergoes diffraction. This wave character of electrons is used in the making of:
questions.explanation: An electron microscope uses an 'electron beam' to produce the image of the object and magnification is obtained by 'electromagnetic fields'; unlike light or optical microscopes, in which 'light waves' are used to produce the image and magnification is obtained by a system of 'optical lenses'.

questions.question: The correct decreasing order of chemical reactivities in terms of their oxidizing properties for F, Cl, O and N is:
questions.question_html: The correct decreasing order of chemical reactivities in terms of their oxidizing properties for F, Cl, O and N is:
questions.explanation:
All these elements are p-block elements.

The oxidizing character of elements increases from left to right across a period because of the presence of vacant d - orbitals in their valence shells. Thus we get the decreasing order of oxidizing properties as F > O > N

Again, the oxidizing character of elements decreases down the group. Thus, we get F > Cl. However, the oxidizing character of O is more than that of Cl ie. O > Cl

Hence, the correct order of chemical reactivities of F, Cl, O, N in terms of their oxidizing properties is F > O > Cl > N.

questions.question: The standard electrode potential for a couple is +0.15 V and that for the ${\text{C}}{{\text{r}}^{{\text{3}} + }}$ /Cr couple is –0.74 V. These two couples in their standard state are connected to make a cell. The cell potential will be________.
questions.question_html: The standard electrode potential for a couple is +0.15 V and that for the {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}^{{\text{3}} + }}{/tex}/Cr couple is –0.74 V. These two couples in their standard state are connected to make a cell. The cell potential will be________.
questions.explanation:
E_(cell )= E_(cathod) - E_(anode)

Anodic half cell will be Cr^(3+)/Cr.

The cathodic half cell will be Sn^(4+)/Sn^(2+).

So, E_(cell )= 0.15+0.74 = +0.89 V

questions.question: Rocket fuel used in space research is ________.
questions.question_html: Rocket fuel used in space research is ________.
questions.explanation:
Rocket Fuel used in space research is H_(2) Because of the following reasons:

 High specific impulse: In combination with liquid oxygen, it produces the highest specific impulse.
 High exhaust velocity: This is the single most important performance goal and is related to the heat of combustion of the fuel and the molecular mass of the combustion products. Hydrogen surpasses all other chemical fuels in exhaust velocity.
 High reaction rate: Rapid reaction of fuel and oxidizer over a wide range of conditions is advantageous in converting the energy content of chemical fuel to heat in a minimum volume. Hydrogen has high flame speed, low ignition energy, and wide flammability limits.
 Combustion chamber cooling: Combustion chamber can be regeneratively cooled by liquid hydrogen being a cryogenic fuel.

HS^(-), BF_(3), C_(2)H_(5)O^(-), (CH_(3))_(3)N:, Cl^(+), CH_(3)C^(+) = O, H_(2)N^(-):, NO_(2)^(​​​​+)

Select the nucleophile(s) among the following molecules/ions
HS-, BF3, C2H5O-, (CH3)3N:, Cl+, CH3C+ = O, H2N-:, NO2​​​​+
questions.explanation: Nucleophiles are nucleus loving species. These can be negatively charged or netural having lone pair of electrons. So among the given options nucleophiles are HS^(-), H_(2)N^(-):, (CH_(​​​​​3))_(3)N:, C_(2)H_(5)O^(-)

questions.question: For an ionic compound with the tetrahedral structure the ratio $\frac{{r^+}}{{r^{ - }}}$ lies in the range of_____.
questions.question_html: For an ionic compound with the tetrahedral structure the ratio {tex}\frac{{r^+}}{{r^{ - }}}{/tex} lies in the range of_____.
questions.explanation: For the tetrahedral structure of an ionic solid, $\frac{{{r^+ }}}{{{r- }}}$ lies in the range of 0.225 to 0.414.

questions.question: The commonly occurring isotopes of carbon atoms are: _(6)^(12)C, _(6)^(13)C, _(6)^(14)C . They contain:
questions.question_html: The commonly occurring isotopes of carbon atoms are: 612C, 613C, 614C . They contain:
questions.explanation:
Isotopes are atoms with the same number of protons but that have a different number of neutrons.

Atomic number of carbon = 6

So number of protons in each isotope = 6

Number of neutrons in $^{12}_6C$ = 12 - 6 = 6

Number of neutrons in $^{13}_6C$ = 13 - 6 = 7

Number of neutrons in $^{14}_6C$ = 14 - 6 = 8

questions.question: The hybridization state of a carbocation is:
questions.question_html: The hybridization state of a carbocation is:
questions.explanation: The carbocations have trigonal planar shape with positively charged carbon being sp^(2) hybridised. Thus, the shape of a carbocation may be considered as being derived from the overlap of three equivalent C(sp^(2)) hybridised orbitals.

questions.question: Water may be softened using:
questions.question_html: Water may be softened using:
questions.explanation:
Hardness in water is due to the presence of carbonates and bicarbonates of Calcium or Magnesium (when it is temporary hardness) or sulphates and chlorides of Calcium and Magnesium (when it is permanent hardness).

 A complex salt called sodium aluminium silicate (zeolite) is used to remove permanent hardness in water.
 Graham's Salt. Sodium Hexametaphosphate is used as a sequestrant and a water softener.

questions.question: The principle of partition chromatography is:
questions.question_html: The principle of partition chromatography is:

options.text: separation of a mixture over a column of the adsorbent.
options.html: separation of a mixture over a column of the adsorbent.

options.text: separation of substances of a mixture over a thin layer of an adsorbent.
options.html: separation of substances of a mixture over a thin layer of an adsorbent.

options.text: separation of ions and polar molecules based on their affinity to ion exchanger.
options.html: separation of ions and polar molecules based on their affinity to ion exchanger.

options.text: continuous differential partitioning of components of a mixture.
options.html: continuous differential partitioning of components of a mixture.

correct_options: continuous differential partitioning of components of a mixture.
questions.explanation: Partition chromatography is based on the continuous differential partitioning of components of a mixture between stationary and mobile phases. Paper chromatography is a type of partition chromatography. In paper chromatography, a special quality paper known as chromatography paper is used. Chromatography paper contains water trapped in it, which acts as the stationary phase.

questions.explanation: An important feature of this model is that the mass of the atom is assumed to be uniformly distributed over the atom. questions.explanation: The electron releasing tendency of the metals, zinc, copper, and silver is in the order Zn > Cu > Ag. This is according to the metal activity series or electrochemical series.

questions.question: Bond order of ${\text{O}}_2^ + $ and ${\text{O}}_2^ - $ are:
questions.question_html: Bond order of {tex}{\text{O}}_2^ + {/tex} and {tex}{\text{O}}_2^ - {/tex} are:
questions.explanation:
The electronic configuration of the O_(2)^(+ )ion containing 15 electrons can be written as: $\sigma $ 1s^(2)< $\sigma $ *1s^(2)< $\sigma $ 2s^(2)< $\sigma $ *2s^(2)< $\sigma $ 2p_(z)^(2) < ( $\pi $ 2p_(x)^(2)= $\pi $ 2p_(y)^(2)) < ( $\pi $ *2p_(x)^(1)= $\pi $ *2p_(y)) < $\sigma $ *2p_(z)

The bond order can be found as:

B.O = $N_b-N_a\over 2$ 

N_(b)= Number of electrons in the bonding orbitals = 10

N_(a) = Number of electron in the anti-bonding orbitals = 5

B.O = ^( ${10-5\over 2 }= {5\over 2 }$ )= 2.5

The electronic configuration of the O_(2)^(-) ion containing 17 electrons can be written as: $\sigma $ 1s^(2)< $\sigma $ *1s^(2)< $\sigma $ 2s^(2)< $\sigma $ *2s^(2)< $\sigma $ 2p_(z)^(2) < ( $\pi $ 2p_(x)^(2) = $\pi $ 2p_(y)^(2)) < ( $\pi $ *2p_(x)^(2)= $\pi $ *2p_(y)^(1)) < σ*2p_(z)

The bond order can be calculated as: B.O = ${10-7\over 2} ={ 3\over 2}$ = 1.5

questions.question: The bond that undergoes heterolytic cleavage most readily is:
questions.question_html: The bond that undergoes heterolytic cleavage most readily is:
questions.explanation: Due to the electronegativity difference between O and H atom, the O-H bond gets polarized and rapidly breaks to give H^(+) and O^(2)^(-) ions.

questions.question:
For the reaction

2N_(2)H_(4)(l) + N_(2)O_(4)(l) $\rightarrow$ 3N_(2) (g) + 4H_(2)O (l),

Which is the limiting reagent for this reaction?
questions.question_html:
For the reaction
2N2H4(l) + N2O4(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3N2 (g) + 4H2O (l),
Which is the limiting reagent for this reaction?
questions.explanation:
For the given reaction,

2N_(2)H_(4 )(l) + N_(2)O_(4 )(l) $\rightarrow$ 3N_(2) (g) + 4H_(2)O (l)

as per its stoichiometry, if only the right and exact amount of N_(2)O_(4) is taken, the reaction may not go to completion. As such it would be consumed first & is, therefore, the limiting reagent.

questions.question: Rank the bonds in the set C=O, C-O, C $ \equiv $ O in order of decreasing bond strength.
questions.question_html: Rank the bonds in the set C=O, C-O, C{tex} \equiv {/tex}O in order of decreasing bond strength.
questions.explanation: The more bonds between atoms, the shorter the bond is. For example, a triple bond will be shorter than a double bond. The shorter the bond is the stronger it is (higher dissociation energy). Bond order is basically the number of bonds between atoms, so a larger number bond order=more bonds=shorter bonds=stronger bonds. i.e., C $ \equiv $ O > C=O > C-O.

questions.explanation: Bond angles also contribute to the shape of a molecule. Bond angles are the angles between adjacent lines representing bonds. The bond angle can help differentiate between linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal-bipyramidal, and octahedral. The ideal bond angles are the angles that demonstrate the maximum angle where it would minimize repulsion, thus verifying the VSEPR theory.

Elements of group 7, 8, 9 of d – block do not form hydrides at all. This inability of metal, of group 7, 8, 9 of periodic table to form hydrides is referred to as hydride gap of d – block.

questions.question: The manganese (Z=25) has the outer configuration:
questions.question_html: The manganese (Z=25) has the outer configuration:
questions.explanation:
3d^(5)4s^(2)

The 3d orbital in this case is exactly half-filled and the 4s orbital is completely filled. The half filled and completely filled electronic configurations have a symmetrical distribution of electrons which leads to stability. The 4s sub-energy level is at lower energy than the 3d sub-energy level.

questions.question: A balanced chemical equation has the same number of atoms of each element on both sides of the equation. This is in accordance with one of the following laws

options.text: Gay Lussac's Law of gaseous volumes
options.html: Gay Lussac's Law of gaseous volumes

questions.explanation: As a result of the law of conservation of mass ( ie." matter can neither be created nor destroyed " ), it is significant that in any chemical change the total mass of the reacting substances should be equal to that of the products formed. This is easily represented through a balanced chemical equation for the reaction being considered. Further, since the number of atoms of different elements, with their own atomic masses, are involved in a chemical change their number should not change ( or remain the same ) even before and after the reaction, irrespective of the compound/element used as a reactant or in products yielded.

questions.question: Maximum number of electrons in a subshell with l = 3 and n = 4 is _____.
questions.question_html: Maximum number of electrons in a subshell with l = 3 and n = 4 is _____.
questions.explanation:
Here l = 3 means the subshell is f. 

n = 4 means it is present in the 4th orbit. Finally, it is a 4f subshell.

The number of orbital in f sub shell = 2l + 1 = 2 $\times$ 3 + 1 = 7

Each orbital can accommodate 2 electrons = 7 $\times$ 2 = 14 electrons.

Therefore, the maximum number of electrons in a subshell with l = 3 and n = 4 (4f) is 14 electrons.

questions.question: Delocalisation of σ electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital is called:
questions.question_html: Delocalisation of σ electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital is called:
questions.explanation: Delocalisation of σ electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital is known as Hyperconjugation. It is a general stabilising interaction. The $\sigma$ electrons of C- H bond of the alkyl group enter into partial conjugation with the attached unsaturated system or with the unshared p orbital. It is a permanent effect.

questions.question: Sodium cyanide, sulphide, and halide, (-CN, -S, and -H, coming from the organic compound) formed on fusion with sodium are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This is called:
questions.question_html: Sodium cyanide, sulphide, and halide, (-CN, -S, and -H, coming from the organic compound) formed on fusion with sodium are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This is called:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus present in an organic compound are detected by “Lassaigne’s test”. The elements present in the compound are converted from the covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. Cyanide, sulphide, and halide of sodium so formed on sodium fusion are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This extract is known as sodium fusion extract.

questions.question:
Consider the reaction,

 $2{C_4}{H_{10}}(g) + 13{O_2}(g) \to 8C{O_2}(g) + 10{H_2}O(l)$ 

If 300g of C_(4)H_(10) (g) is burnt with 1000 g of O_(2), the yield of H_(2)O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are _____ and _____ respectively.
questions.question_html:
Consider the  reaction,
{tex}2{C_4}{H_{10}}(g) + 13{O_2}(g) \to 8C{O_2}(g) + 10{H_2}O(l){/tex}
If 300g of C4H10 (g) is burnt with 1000 g of O2, the yield of H2O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are _____ and _____ respectively.
questions.explanation:
Calculations

As per the stoichiometry of the given equation,

 $2{C_4}{H_{10}}(g) + 13{O_2}(g) \to 8C{O_2}(g) + 10{H_2}O(l)$ 

Calculations:

Step 1/ -

Molar mass of C_(4)H_(10)

= (4 $\times$ 2 + 1 $\times$ 10) amu = (48 + 10) amu

= 58 amu

 $\therefore$ 300g of C_(4)H_(10)

= $\frac{300}{58}$ moles

= 5.17 moles

Step 2 / -

 For the yield of H_(2)O;

 Since 2 mols of C_(4)H_(10) yields 10 H_(2)O i.e.

 = 10 moles.

 $\therefore$ 5.17 moles of C_(4)H_(10) would yield 10H_(2)O_( )

 $ = \frac{{5.17 \times 10}}{2}moles$ 

 = 25.85 moles

 $ = (25.85 \times 18)g$ 

 465.3 g $\approx$ 465 g
 For predicting the limiting reagent;
 
 Calculate the number of moles of O_(2) needed to react with 5.17 moles of C_(4)H_(10)

 Since 2 moles of C_(4)H_(10) requires 13 O_(2) molecules i.e.

 = 13 moles

 $ = \frac{{13 \times 5.17}}{2}moles$ 

 = 33.62 moles

 Number of moles of O_(2 )actually taken for combustion

 = $\frac{mass \ of \ oxygen \ taken }{ molar \ mass \ of \ oxygen}$ 

 = $\frac{1000}{32} moles$ 

 = 31.25 moles

 Thus, it is noteworthy that,

 O_(2(g)) taken for reaction (i.e. 31.25 moles) is lesser than the required number of moles (i.e. 33.62 moles).

 Hence, it would be consumed first and is, therefore, the limiting reagent for this reaction.

questions.question: According to the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, in dealing with milligram-sized or heavier objects:
questions.question_html: According to the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, in dealing with milligram-sized or heavier objects:

options.text: there are no uncertainties at all
options.html: there are no uncertainties at all

options.text: the associated uncertainties are very important
options.html: the associated uncertainties are very important

options.text: the associated uncertainties are hardly of any real consequence
options.html: the associated uncertainties are hardly of any real consequence

correct_options: the associated uncertainties are hardly of any real consequence
questions.explanation:
The effect of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle is significant only for motion of microscopic objects, it is negligible in case of macroscopic objects. For example, we apply the concept of uncertainty to an object of mass 1 milligram.

 $\Delta x \times \Delta v_x = {h\over 4\pi m}$ 

 $={ 6.626\times 10^{−34}\over 4\times 3.1416\times 10^{−6}}≈10^{−28}m^2s^{−1}$ 

The value of $\Delta x\Delta v_x $ is very small and insignificant in this case. Therefore, when we deal with heavier objects the associated uncertainties have no real consequences and can be neglected.

questions.question: In Duma's method for estimation of nitrogen, 0.3 g of an organic compound gave 50 mL of nitrogen collected at 300K temperature and 715 mm pressure. The percentage composition of nitrogen in the compound (Aqueous tension at 300K = 15mm) is
questions.question_html: In Duma's method for estimation of nitrogen, 0.3 g of an organic compound gave 50 mL of nitrogen collected at 300K temperature and 715 mm pressure. The percentage composition of nitrogen in the compound (Aqueous tension at 300K = 15mm) is
questions.explanation:
Volume of nitrogen collected at 300 K and 715 mm Hg= 50 mL

Actual pressure = 715 - 15 = 700 mm Hg

Volume of nitrogen at STP = $\frac {(273 \times 700 \times 50)}{(300 \times 760)}$ = 41.9 mL

22400 ml of nitrogen at STP weighs 28g

So 41.9 ml of nitrogen at STP weighs = $\frac {(28 g \times 41.9mL)}{22400}$ mL

Percentage of N_(2) = $\frac {(28 \times 41.9 \times 100)}{(22400\times0.3)}$ =17.46%

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
Atoms and molecules are extremely small in size and their numbers in even a small amount of any substance are really very large. To handle such large numbers, a unit of convenient magnitude is required. The idea of the mole is to count entities at the microscopic level (i.e., atoms, molecules, particles, electrons, ions, etc). In the SI system, mole (symbol, mol) was introduced as the seventh base quantity for the amount of a substance. The mole, symbol mol, is the SI unit of the amount of substance. One mole contains exactly 6.02214076 $\times$ 10^(23) elementary entities. This number is the fixed numerical value of the Avogadro constant, N_(A), when expressed in the unit mol^(–1) and is called the Avogadro number. The amount of substance, symbol n, of a system is a measure of the number of specified elementary entities. An elementary entity may be an atom, a molecule, an ion, an electron, any other particle or specified group of particles. the empirical formula represents the simplest whole-number ratio of various atoms present in a compound, whereas, the molecular formula shows the exact number of different types of atoms present in a molecule of a compound.

The mass of 1 mole of a substance in grams is known as

The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.

 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: Mendeleev proposed that some of the elements were still undiscovered and, therefore, left several gaps in the table. He left the gap under aluminium and a gap under silicon and called these elements Eka-Aluminium and Eka-Silicon. These were later discovered and named.
questions.question_html: Mendeleev proposed that some of the elements were still undiscovered and, therefore, left several gaps in the table. He left the gap under aluminium and a gap under silicon and called these elements Eka-Aluminium and Eka-Silicon. These were later discovered and named.
questions.explanation: Mendeleev arranged the elements according to their atomic weights. He corrected the atomic weights of a few existing elements to explain their chemical and physical behaviour. Moreover, he felt that few elements are still to be discovered, so he left empty spaces for them in his periodic table. Two such elements whose properties he predicted; and were later discovered are Gallium and Germanium respectively.

questions.question: Which of the following oxides is neutral?
questions.question_html: Which of the following oxides is neutral?
questions.explanation: CO(carbon monoxide) has a triple covalent bound between C and O and therefore no ionization of any kind. The covalent character of carbon means that there is no tendency for carbon to ionize on its own to form C^(4+) or C^(4-), hence CO stays as neutral oxide.

questions.question: Oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously, hence, the word “redox” was coined for this class of chemical reactions. questions.question: Hydrogen peroxide is used as:
questions.question_html: Hydrogen peroxide is used as:
questions.explanation:
Hydrogen peroxide (H_(2)O_(2)) is a very pale blue liquid that appears colourless in a dilute solution, slightly more viscous than water. It is a weak acid. It has strong oxidizing properties and is, therefore, a powerful bleaching agent that is mostly used for bleaching paper but has also found use as a disinfectant and as an oxidizer.

Hydrogen peroxide in acid solution is oxidized with KMnO_(4) and reduced with KI. When H_(2)O_(2) serves as an oxidizing agent, the oxygen is reduced to H_(2)O. When H_(2)O_(2) serves as a reducing agent, the oxygen is oxidized to O_(2) and bubbles are noticed.

questions.question_html: The typical range of molar enthalpies for the strongest intermolecular (Hydrogen) bonds is:
questions.explanation: A hydrogen bond is an electrostatic attraction between two polar groups that occurs when a hydrogen (H) atom linked to a small-sized highly electronegative atom such as nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), or fluorine (F). Therefore unlike a covalent bond, it has very low dissociation energy with a range of molar enthalpy = (4 - 25) kJ.

questions.question: In the Lassaigne's test for nitrogen in an organic compound, the Prussian blue colour is obtained due to the formation of :
questions.question_html: In the Lassaigne's test for nitrogen in an organic compound, the Prussian blue colour is obtained due to the formation of :
questions.explanation: In Lassaigne's test, the sodium fusion extract is boiled with iron(II) sulphate and then acidified with concentrated sulphuric acid. The formation of Prussian blue colour confirms the presence of nitrogen. Sodium cyanide first reacts with iron(II)sulphate and forms sodium hexacyanoferrate(II). On heating with concentrated sulphuric acid some iron(II) ions are oxidised to iron(III) ions which react with sodium hexacyanoferrate(II) to produce iron(III) hexacyanoferrate(II) (ferriferrocyanide) which is Prussian blue in colour. Thus the prussion blue colour is due the formation of Fe_(4)[Fe(CN)_(6)]_(3)

questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of a tennis ball of mass 60 g moving with a velocity of 10 metres per second is approximately:
questions.question_html: The de Broglie wavelength of a tennis ball of mass 60 g moving with a velocity of 10 metres per second is approximately:
questions.explanation:
The mass of the ball = 60 g = 6 $\times$ 10^(-2) kg

Velocity of the ball = 10m/s

let the de broglie wavelength be $\lambda $ .

We know, $\lambda={h\over mv}$ 

 $\lambda ={6.626\times 10^{-34}\over6\times 10^{-2}\times 10}$ = $1.103\times 10^{-33}\approx 10^{-33}m$

questions.question: Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound ascertained on the basis that electron in a covalent bond belongs
questions.question_html: Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound ascertained on the basis that electron in a covalent bond belongs

options.text: entirely to transition elements if present
options.html: entirely to transition elements if present

options.text: entirely to halogens in a compound
options.html: entirely to halogens in a compound

questions.explanation:
Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a compound according to some rules. This number enable us to describe oxidation-reduction reactions, and balancing redox chemical reactions. When a covalent bond forms between two atoms with different electronegativities the shared electrons in the bond lie closer to the more electronegative atom eg. HCl

The oxidation number of an atom is the charge that results when the electrons in a covalent bond are assigned to the more electronegative atom. It is the charge on an atom would possess if the bonding were ionic. In HCl (above) the oxidation number for the hydrogen would be +1 and that of the Cl would be -1

questions.question:
Consider the unbalanced chemical equation ,

-- Al + -- $O_2$ → -- $Al_2O_3$ .

The equation for the above reaction can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces.
questions.question_html:
Consider the unbalanced  chemical equation ,
-- Al + -- {tex}O_2{/tex} → -- {tex}Al_2O_3{/tex}.
The equation for the above reaction can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces.
questions.explanation:
The unbalanced chemical reaction is 

Al(s) + O_(2)​(g) $\longrightarrow$ Al_(2)​O_(3)​(s)

Balance Al atoms by adding 2 to Al on LHS

2Al(s) + O_(2)​(g) $\longrightarrow$ Al_(2)​O_(3)​(s)

Balance O atoms by adding $\frac 32$ ​ to O_(2)​ on LHS

2Al(s) + ​ $\frac 32$ O_(2)​(g) $\longrightarrow$ Al_(2)​O_(3)​(s)

Multiply the whole equation with 2.

4Al(s) + 3O_(2)​(g) $\longrightarrow$ 2Al_(2)​O_(3)​(s)

questions.explanation: Perhydrol is a solution of hydrogen peroxide (H_(2)O_(2)) at a concentration of 30% of weight by volume(w/v) or 100 volume strength. Perhydrol is a mild antiseptic used on the skin to prevent infection of minor cuts, scrapes, and burns. It may also be used as a mouth rinse to help remove mucus or to relieve minor mouth irritation (e.g., due to canker/ cold sores, gingivitis). This product works by releasing oxygen when it is applied to the affected area. The release of oxygen causes foaming, which helps to remove dead skin and clean the area.

questions.question: Rutherford’s $\alpha$ –particle scattering experiment supported the discovery of:
questions.question_html: Rutherford’s {tex}\alpha{/tex}–particle scattering experiment supported the discovery of:
questions.explanation:
Rutherford's model of an atom: Ernest Rutherford was interested in knowing how the electrons are arranged within an atom. Rutherford designed an experiment for this. In this experiment, fast-moving alpha ( $\alpha$ )-particles were made to fall on a thin gold foil.

On the basis of his experiment, Rutherford put forward the model of an atom, which had the following features:

 There is a positively charged centre in an atom called the nucleus.
 Nearly all the mass of an atom resides in the nucleus. The electrons revolve around the nucleus in well-defined orbits.
 The size of the nucleus is very small as compared to the size of the atom.

Therefore, the Molarity of NaOH solution is 0.4 M.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
In 1830, Michael Faraday showed that if electricity is passed through a solution of an electrolyte, chemical reactions occurred at the electrodes, which resulted in the liberation and deposition of matter at the electrodes. In the mid-1850s Faraday began to study electrical discharge in partially evacuated tubes, known as cathode ray discharge tubes. When sufficiently high voltage is applied across the electrodes, current starts flowing through a stream of particles moving in the tube from the negative electrode to the positive electrode. These were called cathode rays or cathode ray particles. J.J. Thomson measured the ratio of electrical charge (e) to the mass of the electron (m_(e)) by using a cathode ray tube and applying electrical and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other as well as to the path of electrons. Positively charged particle was characterised in 1919. Later, a need was felt for the presence of electrically neutral particles as one of the constituents of the atom.

Assertion (A): The cathode rays start from cathode and move towards the anode.

Reason (R): In the absence of an electrical or magnetic field, cathode rays travel in straight lines.
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:

In 1830, Michael Faraday showed that if electricity is passed through a solution of an electrolyte, chemical reactions occurred at the electrodes, which resulted in the liberation and deposition of matter at the electrodes. In the mid-1850s Faraday began to study electrical discharge in partially evacuated tubes, known as cathode ray discharge tubes.  When sufficiently high voltage is applied across the electrodes, current starts flowing through a stream of particles moving in the tube from the negative electrode to the positive electrode. These were called cathode rays or cathode ray particles. J.J. Thomson measured the ratio of electrical charge (e) to the mass of the electron (me) by using a cathode ray tube and applying electrical and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other as well as to the path of electrons. Positively charged particle was characterised in 1919. Later, a need was felt for the presence of electrically neutral particles as one of the constituents of the atom.


Assertion (A): The cathode rays start from cathode and move towards the anode.
Reason (R): In the absence of an electrical or magnetic field, cathode rays travel in straight lines.

Rest option are all the members of 5f-inner transition series, also known as the actinoid series i.e. Curium ([Rn] 5f^(7) 6d^(1) 7s^(2)) ; Californium ([Rn] 5f^(10) 7s_(2)); and Uranium ([Rn] 5f^(3) 6d^(1) 7s^(2)).

Which of the following statements is false?

 Alkali metals form covalent bonds with oxygen.
 Alkali metals form oxides that act as basic anhydrides.
 Alkali metals usually have a +1 oxidation state.
 Alkali metals have relatively low first ionization energies.
questions.question_html:
Which of the following statements is false?

  1. Alkali metals form covalent bonds with oxygen.
  2. Alkali metals form oxides that act as basic anhydrides.
  3. Alkali metals usually have a +1 oxidation state.
  4. Alkali metals have relatively low first ionization energies.
questions.explanation: Alkali metals have lowest ionization energies in their respective periods. They prefer to loose an electron to form a cation and thereby form an ionic compound.

questions.question: Which one of the following pairs of species has the same bond order?
questions.question_html: Which one of the following pairs of species has the same bond order?
questions.explanation:
Bond order= ${n_b−n_a\over 2}$ 

In CN^(-), there are 8 bonding electrons and 2 antibonding electrons,

Therefore B.O.= $8−2\over 2$ = 3

NO^(+) has 10 electrons, a triple bond between N and O, and two pairs, one on each atom. This means that both N and O follow the octet rule. The bond order is 3.

questions.question: The giant planets Jupiter and Saturn consist mostly of ________.
questions.question_html: The giant planets Jupiter and Saturn consist mostly of ________.
questions.explanation:
The name ‘gas giant’ was coined in 1952 by science fiction writer James Blish to refer to all the giant planets. The giant planets (Jupiter and Saturn) are huge gas balls, which is quite different than Earth and the other three rocky inner planets. Gas giants are primarily composed of gases, such as hydrogen and helium, and a much thicker layer of metallic hydrogen, along with a molten rocky core.

Unlike rocky planets, gas giants do not have a well-defined surface, and there is no clear boundary between where the atmosphere ends and the surface begins. In other words, you wouldn’t be able to land on these planets, as their atmospheres simply become gradually denser towards the core, perhaps with liquid or liquid-like states somewhere in between.

questions.question: According to Gay Lussac’s Law of Gaseous Volumes, when gases combine or produced in a chemical reaction they do so in
questions.question_html: According to Gay Lussac’s Law of Gaseous Volumes, when gases combine or produced in a chemical reaction they do so in

options.text: a ratio of 1:1 by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.
options.html: a ratio of 1:1 by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.

options.text: the inverse ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.
options.html: the inverse ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.

options.text: a simple ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.
options.html: a simple ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.

options.text: a ratio of 1:2 by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.
options.html: a ratio of 1:2 by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.

correct_options: a simple ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.
questions.explanation:
The given statement for Gaylussac's law of gaseous volume is correct. The law can be exemplified and understood easily as below-

Experimentally it has been observed that 100mL of hydrogen combine with 50 mL of oxygen to produce 100 mL of water vapour

 $\mathop {{H_2}(g)}\limits_{100\;mL} + \mathop {{O_2}(g)}\limits_{50\;mL} \to \mathop {{H_2}O(g)}\limits_{100\;mL} $ 

Thus the volumes of hydrogen and oxygen which combine together ( ie. 100mL and 50 mL ) bear a simple ratio of 2 : 1

questions.question: N_(2), CO, and NO^(+ )are isoelectronic molecules. Their respective bond order is:
questions.question_html: N2, CO, and NO+ are isoelectronic molecules. Their respective bond order is:
questions.explanation:
Total number of electrons in N_(2) molecule is 7+ 7= 14.

As per the formula Bonded pair of electrons N_(b): $σ1s^2\ σ2s^2\ $\pi$ 2p_y^2\ $\pi$ 2p_z^2\ σ 2p_x^2$ 

Total 10 electrons.

Anti bond pairs of electrons N_(a): $σ 1s*^2\ σ 2s*^2$ Total 4 electrons.

Bond Order (B.O.) = ${N_b-N_a\over 2} ={ 10-4\over 2} = 3 $ 

Similarly bond order of CO And NO^(+) is 3

questions.question: For the reaction, 2 N_(2)H_(4) (l) + N_(2)O_(4 )(l) $\to $ 3N_(2)(g) + 4H_(2)O(l), if 160 g of N_(2)H_(4 )is mixed with 160 g N_(2)O_(4), the yield of H_(2)O is:
questions.question_html: For the reaction, 2 N2H4 (l) + N2O4 (l) {tex}\to {/tex} 3N2(g) + 4H2O(l), if 160 g of N2H4 is mixed with 160 g N2O4, the yield of H2O is:
questions.explanation:
Calculations:

Moles of N_(2)H_(4)

= $\frac{160}{32}$ 

= 5 moles 

Moles of N_(2)O_(4)

= $\frac{160}{92}$ 

= 1.739 moles

As per stoichiometry of the given reaction,

2 moles of N_(2)H_(4) require 1 mole of N_(2)O_(4),

 $\therefore$ 5 mole of N_(2)H_(4) will need 2.5 moles of N_(2)O_(4)

. But we have only 1.73 mole of N_(2)O_(4) so it would be consumed first.

Thus N_(2)O_(4) is the limiting reagent for this reaction.

Again, since 1 mole of N_(2)O_(4 )yields water

= 4 mole of water

 $ \therefore$ 1.73 mole of N_(2)O_(4) will yield water

= 6.92 moles

 $\equiv $ (6.92 $\times$ 18) g

= 124.56g of water

questions.question_html: What will be the Electronic configuration of the element having atomic number 24?
questions.explanation: The atomic number 24 is of Cr. Due to half-filled orbital stability, it does not follow the Aufbau rule so its electronic configuration is 1s^(2) 2s^(2) 2p^(6) 3s^(2) 3p^(6) 3d^(5) 4s^(1).

questions.question: In which of the following compounds, an element exhibits two different oxidation states?
questions.question_html: In which of the following compounds, an element exhibits two different oxidation states?
questions.explanation:
NH_(4)NO_(3) is an ionic compound consisting of NH_(4)^(+) and NO_(3)^(-) ion. The oxidation number of N in two species is different as shown below:

In the case of NH_(4)^(+),

Let the oxidation state of N in NH_(4)^(+ )be x.

x + 4 $\times$ (+1) = +1

x = -3

In the NO_(3)^(-)_(,)

Let the oxidation state of N in NO_(3)^(- )be y,

y + 3 $\times$ (-2) = -1

y = +5

Hence, NH_(4)NO_(3 )exhibits two different oxidation states.

questions.question: The molar mass of C_(6) H_(10) O_(5 ) is
questions.question_html: The molar mass of   C6 H10 O5  is
questions.explanation:
Molar massof C_(6 )H_(10) O_(5)

= Sum of the atomic masses of six C atoms , ten H atoms & six O atoms

thus , substituting the required atomic masses of

 C= 12 ,

H = 1 , &

 O = 16 ,

 we get ,

Molar mass of C_(6) H_(10) O_(5) 

 = [ 6(12)+10(1)+5(16) ] u

= (72 + 10 + 80 ) u

=162 u

questions.question: Does the term reduction have one of the following options taking place?
questions.question_html: Does the term reduction have one of the following options taking place?

options.text: Removal of oxygen from a substance
options.html: Removal of oxygen from a substance

options.text: Removal of the electropositive element from a substance
options.html: Removal of the electropositive element from a substance

options.text: Removal of hydrogen to a substance
options.html: Removal of hydrogen to a substance

options.text: Addition of an electronegative element to a substance
options.html: Addition of an electronegative element to a substance

correct_options: Removal of oxygen from a substance
questions.explanation:
The reduction is addition of hydrogen, removal of oxygen, removal of an electronegative element or addition of an electropositive element.

 Removal of Oxygen: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which oxygen is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).

 CuO + C $\longrightarrow$ Cu + CO

 H_(2)O + C $\longrightarrow$ CO + H_(2)

 2CO + O_(2 ) $\longrightarrow$ 2CO_(2)

 Fe_(3)O_(4) + 4H_(2) $\longrightarrow$ 3F_(2) + 4H_(2)O
 Addition of Hydrogen: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which hydrogen is added to any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).

 Cl_(2) + H_(2) $\longrightarrow$ 2HCl

 S + H_(2) $\longrightarrow$ H_(2)S

 C_(2)H_(4) + H_(2) $\longrightarrow$ C_(2)H_(6)
 Removal of an Electronegative Element: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which an electronegative element is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 2HgCl_(2) + SnCl_(2) $\longrightarrow$ Hg_(2)Cl_(2) + SnCl_(4) (Reduction of mercuric chloride)
 2FeCl_(3) + H_(2) $\longrightarrow$ 2FeCl_(2) + 2HCl (Reduction of ferric chloride)
 2FeCl_(3) + H_(2)S $\longrightarrow$ 2FeCl_(2) + 2HCl + S (Reduction of ferric chloride)
 Addition of an Electropositive Element: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which an electropositive element is added any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).

 HgCl_(2) + Hg $\longrightarrow$ Hg_(2)Cl_(2) (Reduction of mercuric chloride)

 CuCl_(2) + Cu $\longrightarrow$ Cu_(2)Cl_(2) (Reduction of cupric chloride)
 The substance which brings reduction is known as a reducing agent.
 A substance, which undergoes oxidation, acts as a reducing agent while a substance, which undergoes reduction, acts as an oxidizing agent.

Mg, S, Cu, Na_(2)SO_(3), H_(2)S, HI, H_(2), C, KI are reducing agents, while O_(2), Cl_(2), F_(2), H_(2)O_(2), MnO_(2), FeCl_(3), CuCl_(2), Fe_(3)O_(4), CuO, etc., are oxidizing agents in the above examples.

 All oxidation and reduction reactions are complimentary of one another and occur simultaneously, one cannot take place without the other.
 No single oxidation and no single reduction process is known. The simultaneously oxidation and reduction reactions are generally termed as redox reactions. e.g., 2FeCl_(3) + SnCl_(2) $\longrightarrow$ 2FeCl_(2) + SnCl_(4).

In above example iron undergoes reduction from +3 to +2.

questions.explanation: The metallic character depends on the ease with which an atom can loose electron i.e. its the ionisation energy. Lower the ionization energy higher is the metallic character. The ionization energy is of the order K < Mg < Al < B; hence the metallic character is reverse of that K > Mg > Al > B.

questions.explanation: $[Ne]3s^23p^3$ and $[Ar]3d^{10}4s^24p^3$ both have stable half-filled configuration but in $[Ne]3s^23p^3$ the atomic radii are smaller than $[Ar]3d^{10}4s^24p^3$ . So ionization enthalpy of $[Ne]3s^23p^3$ is highest amongst all.

questions.question:
Consider the reactions:

 H_(2)O_(2) + 2HI $\to$ I_(2) + 2H_(2)O
 HOCl + H_(2)O_(2) $\to$ H_(3)O + Cl + O_(2) 

Which of the following statements is correct about H_(2)O_(2) with reference to these reactions? Hydrogen perioxide is:
questions.question_html:
Consider the reactions:

  1. H2O2 + 2HI {tex}\to{/tex} I2 + 2H2O
  2. HOCl + H2O2 {tex}\to{/tex} H3O + Cl + O2 

Which of the following statements is correct about H2O2 with reference to these reactions? Hydrogen perioxide is:

options.text: an oxidising agent in both A and B
options.html: an oxidising agent in both A and B

options.text: a reducing agent in both A and B
options.html: a reducing agent in both A and B

options.text: an oxidising agent in A and reducing agent in B
options.html: an oxidising agent in A and reducing agent in B

options.text: a reducing agent in A and oxidising agent in B
options.html: a reducing agent in A and oxidising agent in B

correct_options: an oxidising agent in A and reducing agent in B
questions.explanation: H_(2)O_(2) is an oxidizing agent in 1st reaction and Reducing agent in 2nd reaction.

questions.question: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in H_(2)C = O and mention the molecular geometry also.
questions.question_html: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in H2C = O and mention the molecular geometry also.
questions.explanation: Due to the presence of a double bond in the carbonyl carbon ( $>\mathrm C=\mathrm O$ ), carbon is sp^(2) hybridised. This hybridization leads to a trigonal planar geometry which means a bond angle of 120° around the C.

questions.question: The formula E= h $\nu $ is used to calculate:
questions.question_html: The formula E= h{tex}\nu {/tex}is used to calculate:

options.text: radiation emitted by a black body
options.html: radiation emitted by a black body

questions.explanation:
Max Planck theorized that energy was transferred in chunks known as quanta, equal to $h\nu$ . The variable h is a constant equal to 6.63 $\times$ 10^(-34) J·s and the variable $\nu $ represents the frequency in 1/s.

This equation allows us to calculate the energy of photons, given their frequency.

If the wavelength is given, the energy can be determined by first using the wave equation $ (c = \lambda \times \nu) $ to find the frequency, then using Planck’s equation to calculate energy.

questions.question: Water undergoes self ionization to a small extent to give:
questions.question_html: Water undergoes self ionization to a small extent to give:
questions.explanation:
Water molecules collide with one another to cause the self-ionization reaction represented by this equation:

 $2H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_3O^+ + OH^- $ 

It is a reversible reaction so the equation is usually written with the arrows going in both directions.

The reaction does not form very much H_(3)O^(+) or OH^(-). In one liter of water there are about 55 moles of water molecules, but only 1.0 $\times$ 10^(-7) moles of H_(3)O^(+) and OH^(-) are formed (at room temperature). So the concentrations of H_(3)O^(+) and OH^(-) in pure water are 1.0 $\times$ 10^(-7) M.

questions.question:
When excess of CO is used for the reaction,

Fe_(2) O_(3(s) )+ 3CO_(()_(g)) $\longrightarrow$ 2Fe + 3CO_(2) 

the limiting reagent is_____.
questions.question_html:
When excess of  CO is used for the reaction,
Fe2 O3(s) + 3CO(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}   2Fe + 3CO2 
the limiting reagent is_____.
questions.explanation:
As per stoichiometry of the reaction,

Fe_(2) O_(3(s) )+ 3CO_(()_(g)) $\longrightarrow$ 2Fe + 3CO_(2) 

Fe_(2) O_(3) is the limiting reagent for the given reaction as it would be consumed first, because the other reagent ie. CO_((g)) is used in excess.
